5. **Review request template** — the exact text message I send to a client asking for a Google review. Under 50 words. Include the direct review link format.

Do NOT suggest I change my business name to include keywords — that violates Google's guidelines.
Do NOT write a description that reads like a resume. It should speak to the client's needs.
Do NOT skip the Q&A section — this directly impacts local search visibility.
quickStart: Write my Google Business Profile description (750 chars), 8 service listings, and 10 Q&A entries for a real estate agent in [MARKET] specializing in [NICHE]. Make it keyword-rich but natural.

title: Neighborhood Guide Writer
bestFor: ** Agents wanting to dominate local search results
prompt:
You are a local real estate content writer. Write a comprehensive neighborhood guide.

Neighborhood: [NAME]
City: [CITY]
What I know about this neighborhood: [SHARE EVERYTHING — price ranges, vibe, demographics, schools, restaurants, parks, walkability, transit, etc.]

Write a 1,200-word neighborhood guide with these exact sections:

1. **Overview** (150 words) — the "elevator pitch" for this neighborhood
2. **Who Lives Here** (100 words) — typical residents, demographics, vibe
3. **Housing Stock & Prices** (150 words) — what types of homes, price ranges, trends
4. **Schools** (100 words) — school district, notable schools, ratings context
5. **Dining & Shopping** (150 words) — specific restaurants, cafes, shops BY NAME
6. **Parks & Recreation** (100 words) — specific parks, trails, facilities BY NAME
7. **Commute & Transit** (100 words) — drive times to major employment centers, transit options
8. **What Locals Love** (100 words) — insider knowledge, hidden gems
9. **What to Know Before You Move Here** (100 words) — honest drawbacks or considerations
10. **CTA** (50 words) — offer to give them a personalized neighborhood tour

Include an SEO title tag and meta description at the top.

Do NOT write anything you can't verify from the details I provided. If I didn't mention it, skip it or flag it as "[VERIFY: ...]" so I can fill it in.
Do NOT write only positive things. Section 9 must include honest considerations — that builds trust.
Do NOT use the words "charming," "vibrant," "bustling," or "hidden gem" (you can describe what makes it those things instead).
quickStart: Write a 1,200-word neighborhood guide for [NEIGHBORHOOD] in [CITY]. Here's what I know: [DETAILS]. Include 10 sections covering housing, schools, dining, parks, commute, and honest drawbacks. SEO-optimized.

title: Video Script: Market Update
bestFor: ** Agents who want to create weekly video content efficiently
prompt:
You are a real estate video script writer. Write a market update video script.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
Here's the data I have for this month:
- Median sale price: $[AMOUNT] (last month: $[AMOUNT])
- Homes sold: [NUMBER] (last month: [NUMBER])
- Average days on market: [NUMBER] (last month: [NUMBER])
- Active listings: [NUMBER] (last month: [NUMBER])
- Any notable trends: [DESCRIBE]

Write a script for a 2-minute video (approximately 280 words spoken).

Format:

**HOOK (0-8 sec, under 20 words):**
"[Exact words — must create curiosity. NOT 'Hey guys, welcome back to my channel']"

**CONTEXT (8-20 sec):**
"[Set up why this data matters to regular people, not agents]"

**THE DATA (20-70 sec):**
"[Present each data point conversationally. After EACH number, explain what it means for a buyer or seller in ONE sentence]"

**THE INSIGHT (70-100 sec):**
"[Your interpretation — what this means for the next 1-3 months. Be specific, take a stance]"

**CTA (100-120 sec, under 25 words):**
"[Drive a specific action — DM, comment, link in bio]"

**Caption for posting:** (under 75 words, includes 1 lead-generating question)

TONE:
DO: Talk like you're explaining the market to a smart friend over coffee.
DON'T: Talk like you're reading a quarterly earnings report.

Do NOT use the phrase "whether you're buying or selling." Pick a side for each data point.
Do NOT present more than 4 data points. Viewers tune out.
Do NOT end with "like and subscribe" — end with a specific question or offer.
quickStart: Write a 2-minute market update video script using this data: [PASTE YOUR DATA]. Hook must create curiosity. Explain each number's impact on buyers or sellers. End with a specific CTA, not "like and subscribe."

title: YouTube SEO Title & Description Writer
bestFor: ** Agents posting videos on YouTube who want search traffic
prompt:
You are a YouTube SEO specialist for local real estate content.

My channel: [CHANNEL NAME or description]
My market: [CITY/AREA]
Video topic: [DESCRIBE THE VIDEO]
Video length: [DURATION]
Target viewer: [DESCRIBE — e.g., "someone searching 'moving to Austin'"]

Give me:

1. **Title options** (5 titles, each under 60 characters):
   Each must include a local keyword. Rank them by SEO potential.

2. **Description** (write the complete description, 200-300 words):
   - First 2 lines: hook + keyword (this shows in search results)
   - Timestamps: [suggest section timestamps based on the video topic]
   - 3 related video suggestions I should make and link to
   - CTA with a lead magnet link placeholder
   - Location tags/keywords in the last 2 lines

3. **Tags** (15 tags): mix of broad, local, and long-tail

4. **Thumbnail text** (3 options, each under 5 words — what text goes ON the thumbnail):

5. **Pinned comment** (under 50 words — designed to generate replies and capture leads):

Do NOT write titles that start with "How to" for every video — vary the format.
Do NOT use tags that have nothing to do with the video topic. YouTube penalizes this.
Do NOT write a description that's just keywords. It should read naturally.
quickStart: Give me 5 SEO-optimized YouTube titles, a 250-word description, 15 tags, and thumbnail text for a video about [TOPIC] in [MARKET]. Target viewer: [DESCRIBE].

title: Blog Post Idea Generator (Quarterly)
bestFor: ** Agents who never know what to write about
prompt:
You are a real estate content strategist. Generate my next quarter's blog post topics.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My niche: [DESCRIBE]
My target client: [DESCRIBE]
Current month: [MONTH/YEAR]
Neighborhoods I cover: [LIST]

Give me 12 blog post ideas (one per week for 12 weeks) in this table:

| Week | Title | Target Keyword | Search Intent (Buy/Sell/Research) | Estimated Difficulty (Easy/Medium/Hard) | Lead Capture CTA |

Requirements:
- At least 3 posts should target buyer keywords
- At least 3 posts should target seller keywords
- At least 3 posts should be neighborhood-specific
- At least 2 posts should be timely/seasonal
- At least 1 post should target a question-format keyword

For each title, write a one-sentence "angle" — what makes THIS post different from the 50 other posts on the same topic.

Example:
Title: "5 Austin Neighborhoods Where Prices Dropped in Q1 2026"
Angle: "While everyone writes about hot neighborhoods, I'll cover the ones cooling off — attracting bargain-hunting buyers."

Do NOT suggest generic topics like "5 Tips for First-Time Buyers" without a local angle.
Do NOT repeat topics I'd have already covered if I've been blogging for a year.
Do NOT give me topics that require data I can't easily access (e.g., "Foreclosure rate analysis by ZIP code").
quickStart: Give me 12 weekly blog post ideas for next quarter. I'm a [NICHE] agent in [MARKET]. Each needs a title, target keyword, and a one-sentence angle explaining what makes it unique. Mix buyer, seller, and neighborhood topics.

title: Review Request & Response System
bestFor: ** Agents with fewer than 50 Google reviews
prompt:
You are an online reputation specialist for real estate.

My name: [YOUR NAME]
My market: [CITY/AREA]
My current review count: [NUMBER] on Google, [NUMBER] on Zillow
My Google review link: [PASTE or "I need help finding it"]

Give me:

1. **3 review request messages** (situations when I should send each):

   **After closing (text message, under 50 words):**
   "[Write the exact text]"

   **2 weeks post-closing (email, under 100 words):**
   Subject: [Write subject line]
   "[Write the email body]"

   **Gentle reminder if they agreed but haven't done it (text, under 30 words):**
   "[Write the exact text]"

2. **5 review response templates** for different star ratings:

   | Rating | Response (under 75 words each) |
   Write each response. Include my market keywords naturally (this helps local SEO).

   - 5-star response #1 (warm, personal)
   - 5-star response #2 (different tone, avoids copy-paste feel)
   - 4-star response (acknowledge, appreciate)
   - 3-star response (address concern professionally)
   - 1-2 star response (empathize, offer to resolve offline)

3. **Negative review recovery protocol** — 4 steps in bullet points.

TONE for requests:
DO: "Would you be open to sharing your experience on Google? It really helps other families like yours find the right agent."
DON'T: "Please leave me a 5-star review! It would mean the world to me!"

Do NOT suggest incentivizing reviews — this violates most platform policies.
Do NOT write responses that are generic "thank you" messages. Reference something specific (I'll customize).
Do NOT skip the negative review protocol — I need it even if I don't have negative reviews yet.
quickStart: Write 3 review request messages (post-closing text, 2-week email, gentle reminder) and 5 review response templates for 1-5 star reviews. I'm [NAME] in [MARKET]. Keep requests casual and responses keyword-rich for SEO.

title: Video Content Repurposing Workflow
bestFor: ** Agents who make videos but only post them once
prompt:
You are a content repurposing specialist for real estate.

I just created this video: [DESCRIBE — topic, length, platform it was made for]

Show me how to turn this ONE video into 10+ pieces of content. Give me a numbered list:

| # | Content Piece | Platform | Format | What to Extract/Create | Time to Create |

Requirements:
- At least 3 short-form video clips (with the timestamp range to cut and a suggested hook)
- At least 2 static social posts (write the full caption)
- At least 1 blog post outline (with H2 subheadings)
- At least 1 email to my list (write it, under 100 words)
- At least 1 quote graphic (the exact quote to pull)
- At least 2 other formats of your choice

For the short-form clips, specify:
- Timestamp range: [start-end]
- New hook for the clip: "[exact words]"
- Platform: [TikTok / Reel / Short]
- Caption: [under 50 words]

Total time to repurpose should be under 2 hours.

Do NOT suggest repurposing that requires professional editing software. Assume I use CapCut or similar.
Do NOT recommend formats I can't create myself (e.g., infographics requiring a designer).
Do NOT include "post it again in 3 months" as a repurposing strategy.
quickStart: I made a [LENGTH] video about [TOPIC]. Show me 10+ content pieces I can create from it, with the exact caption, hook, or outline for each. Total repurposing time should be under 2 hours.

title: Lead Magnet Idea Generator
bestFor: ** Agents who don't have any lead magnets yet
prompt:
You are a lead magnet strategist for real estate.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My niche: [DESCRIBE]
My target client: [DESCRIBE]
My website monthly visitors: approximately [NUMBER]

Give me 8 lead magnet concepts in this table:

| # | Title (A/B Version A) | Title (A/B Version B) | Format (PDF/Checklist/Quiz/Video/Calculator) | Problem It Solves | Creation Difficulty (Easy/Med/Hard) | Lead Quality (Low/Med/High) |

Then rank my top 3 to build first, based on this priority: easiest to create + highest lead quality.

For my #1 pick, give me:
- The landing page headline (under 12 words)
- The 3 bullet points for the landing page (what they'll learn/get)
- The form fields to collect (keep it to 3 maximum)

LEAD MAGNET EXAMPLES:
GOOD: "The [City] Homebuyer's Neighborhood Scorecard — Rate Any Neighborhood in 5 Minutes"
BAD: "Everything You Need to Know About Real Estate"

Do NOT suggest lead magnets that take more than 4 hours to create.
Do NOT suggest quizzes unless I have a tool to build them (mention tool options).
Do NOT suggest "free consultation" as a lead magnet. That's a CTA, not a magnet.
quickStart: Give me 8 lead magnet ideas for a [NICHE] agent in [MARKET]. Table format: title, format, problem solved, creation difficulty, lead quality. Pick my top 3 and write the landing page headline for #1.

title: Lead Magnet Content Writer (Buyer Guide)
bestFor: ** Agents targeting buyer leads
prompt:
You are a real estate content writer. Write a complete homebuyer guide that I'll use as a lead magnet.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My target buyer: [DESCRIBE]
My name: [YOUR NAME]
My brokerage: [BROKERAGE]

Write a complete PDF guide with these exact sections (aim for 12–15 pages total when designed):

**Title page:** Title, subtitle, my name and brokerage, "[YEAR] Edition"

**Page 1: Introduction** (150 words) — why I created this guide, what they'll learn, a promise of value

**Pages 2-3: Step-by-Step Buying Process** (300 words) — the 8 steps from "thinking about it" to "keys in hand," specific to my state's process

**Pages 4-5: Financial Preparation** (300 words) — what they need financially, current down payment myths, quick mortgage primer

**Pages 6-7: [MARKET] Market Overview** (300 words) — current conditions, price trends, what their budget buys them in different areas

**Pages 8-9: Neighborhood Comparison** (table format) — compare 5 neighborhoods with columns: Median Price, Commute to Downtown, School Rating, Walkability, Best For

**Pages 10-11: House Hunting Checklist** (bullet list) — the 20 things to check at every showing

**Page 12: Common Mistakes to Avoid** (200 words) — 5 mistakes, each with a one-sentence fix

**Page 13: About Me + CTA** (100 words) — brief bio and a clear "book a free buyer consultation" CTA

Write ALL content. Mark anything I need to verify with [VERIFY: reason].

Do NOT write generic national content. Everything should reference [MARKET] specifically.
Do NOT use condescending language. These are adults making a major financial decision.
Do NOT make the "About Me" section longer than 100 words. The guide is about them, not me.
quickStart: Write a complete 12-page homebuyer guide for [MARKET]. Include: buying process, financial prep, market overview, neighborhood comparison table (5 neighborhoods), showing checklist, and common mistakes. All content specific to [MARKET].

title: Lead Magnet Content Writer (Home Valuation Report)
bestFor: ** Agents targeting seller leads
prompt:
You are a real estate marketing writer. Write the content for an automated home valuation report lead magnet.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My name: [YOUR NAME]

Write the content for a 4-page home valuation report template that I'll fill in with specific property data for each lead:

**Page 1: Cover + Estimated Value Range**
- Headline:
- Subheadline explaining what this report includes:
- Value presentation format: "[We estimate your home at $[LOW] – $[HIGH] based on recent comparable sales]"
- Disclaimer text (under 30 words):

**Page 2: Comparable Sales Analysis**
- Section introduction (under 50 words — explain what comps are and why they matter):
- Table template:
  | Address | Beds/Baths | SqFt | Sold Price | Sold Date | Similarity to Your Home |
  [3 rows for comps]
- One-paragraph analysis template: "Your home compares favorably/unfavorably because [TEMPLATE LANGUAGE]..."

**Page 3: Market Context**
- "[AREA] Market Snapshot" section with placeholders for:
  | Metric | Current | 6 Months Ago | Trend |
- "What This Means for You" paragraph (under 75 words — template language a seller cares about)

**Page 4: Next Steps + CTA**
- "3 Ways to Maximize Your Home's Value" (3 brief tips, under 150 words total)
- CTA: book a free in-home valuation for a precise number
- My bio (under 50 words)

Do NOT present this as a definitive value — always frame as an "estimated range."
Do NOT make claims about guaranteed sale prices.
Do NOT forget the disclaimer. Sellers will hold you to numbers.
quickStart: Write a 4-page home valuation report template. Page 1: estimated value range. Page 2: comp analysis table. Page 3: market snapshot. Page 4: CTA to book in-home valuation. Include all copy and table formats.

title: Landing Page Copy Writer (Home Valuation)
bestFor: ** Agents driving paid traffic who need a high-converting seller landing page
prompt:
You are a landing page conversion copywriter for real estate.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My target: Homeowners who are curious about their home's value (potential sellers)
My credibility: [e.g., "200+ homes sold" or "15 years in this market" or "5-star Google reviews"]

Write the complete copy for a home valuation landing page:

**Above the fold (what they see without scrolling):**
- Headline (3 A/B test versions, each under 12 words):
  1.
  2.
  3.
- Subheadline (under 25 words):
- Form headline (above the form, under 8 words):
- Form fields: [list exactly which fields]
- Button text (3 A/B test versions):
  1.
  2.
  3.

**Below the fold:**
- "How it works" — 3 steps with icons, each step under 15 words
- Social proof section — write 2 fake-but-realistic testimonial templates I'll replace with real ones. Format: "[Quote]" — [First Name], [Neighborhood]
- Trust badges text (what credentials/stats to display)
- FAQ section — write 4 questions and answers (each answer under 40 words)
- Final CTA — repeat the form or button with urgency

HEADLINE EXAMPLES:
GOOD: "What Would Your Neighbor Pay for Your Home?"
BAD: "Get Your Free Home Valuation Today!"

Do NOT write copy longer than 600 words total for the entire page. Short pages convert better.
Do NOT ask for more than 4 form fields. Address is the minimum; phone is optional.
Do NOT use the word "free" more than twice on the page.
quickStart: Write landing page copy for a home valuation page in [MARKET]. Include: 3 headline options, subheadline, form fields, button text, 3-step "how it works," 4 FAQs, and social proof section. Total copy under 600 words.

title: Landing Page Copy Writer (Buyer Lead Capture)
bestFor: ** Agents who want to capture buyer leads from website traffic
prompt:
You are a landing page copywriter for real estate buyer lead capture.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My target buyer: [DESCRIBE]
My offer: [e.g., "personalized home search" or "off-market listings access" or "buyer guide download"]
My credibility: [DESCRIBE]

Write complete landing page copy:

**Above the fold:**
- Headline (3 options, each under 12 words):
- Subheadline (under 25 words — answer "what do I get and why should I care?"):
- Form fields: [list exactly which ones — name, email, and what else?]
- Button text (3 options):

**Value proposition section:**
"Why register?" — 4 bullet points, each under 15 words. Format:
[Benefit icon placeholder] [Benefit statement]

**Social proof:**
- Stat to display: "[NUMBER] buyers found their home with us in [YEAR]" (I'll fill in)
- One testimonial template

**Objection handling:**
Write answers to these 3 objections that visitors have but don't voice:
1. "Will I get spammed?"
2. "Can't I just use Zillow?"
3. "Am I committing to anything?"
Each answer: under 25 words.

**Final CTA:**
- Urgency-appropriate closing line (under 20 words)
- Repeat button

Do NOT write "Sign up for our newsletter" energy. This should feel like exclusive access.
Do NOT require a phone number above the fold — add it as optional below.
Do NOT promise "dream home" or "perfect home." Promise useful things: access, speed, information.
quickStart: Write buyer lead capture landing page copy for [MARKET]. Offer: [YOUR OFFER]. Include 3 headline options, value props, objection handling for "will I get spammed," and social proof. Under 500 words total.

title: SOI (Sphere of Influence) Outreach System
bestFor: ** Any agent who wants consistent referrals from people who already know them
prompt:
You are a real estate relationship marketing strategist. Build me a monthly sphere of influence (SOI) outreach system.

My sphere size: approximately [NUMBER] people
My sphere segments:
- Close friends & family: [NUMBER]
- Past clients: [NUMBER]
- Professional contacts (lenders, attorneys, contractors, etc.): [NUMBER]
- Acquaintances & community connections: [NUMBER]
- Former colleagues / school contacts: [NUMBER]

My market: [CITY/AREA]
How I prefer to communicate: [e.g., "text mostly, some email, rarely call" or "mix of everything"]

Create a 12-month outreach calendar in this format:

| Month | Segment to Contact | Outreach Method | Message Theme | Specific Message (ready to send) |

Rules:
- Each segment should be contacted at least once per quarter, but NOT with the same type of message each time.
- Mix value-giving (market updates, tips, local recs) with personal touches (check-ins, congrats, holidays) with soft asks (referrals, reviews).
- Only ONE month per quarter should include a referral ask. The other months should be pure value or personal connection.
- Every message must be under 75 words and sound like it came from a real person, not a drip campaign.

After the calendar, give me:
1. **Weekly SOI time block plan** — exactly how to execute this in 30 minutes per week
2. **5 "trigger event" messages** — what to send when someone in my sphere posts about a life change (new baby, job change, engagement, retirement, kids leaving for college)

Do NOT write messages that sound like marketing emails. These go to people who KNOW me.
Do NOT suggest I call everyone every month. That's not sustainable for a sphere of [X] people.
Do NOT use the phrase "Do you know anyone who..." — find more natural ways to surface referrals.
quickStart: I have a sphere of [NUMBER] people (friends, past clients, professional contacts). Build me a 12-month outreach calendar with specific messages for each segment, each month. Keep messages under 75 words. Only ask for referrals once per quarter. Include what to say when someone posts about a life change.

title: Speed-to-Lead Response Templates
bestFor: ** Any agent who takes more than 5 minutes to respond to new leads
prompt:
You are a real estate lead response specialist. The data is clear: agents who respond within 60 seconds are 10x more likely to connect with a lead than agents who wait 30 minutes. I need pre-written response templates I can fire off instantly.

My name: [YOUR NAME]
My market: [CITY/AREA]

Write instant-response templates for each of these lead sources:

1. **Zillow/Realtor.com inquiry** — they asked about a specific property
2. **Website form fill** — they requested a home valuation or buyer guide
3. **Facebook/Instagram ad lead** — they clicked an ad and filled out a form
4. **Social media DM** — they messaged me directly on Instagram or Facebook
5. **Open house sign-in** — follow-up text within 1 hour of the open house
6. **Referral from a past client** — someone gave them my name
7. **Google ad lead** — they searched for an agent and clicked my ad

For each source, provide:
- **Text message** (under 40 words) — this is the first touch, sent within 60 seconds
- **Follow-up email** (under 100 words) — sent within 10 minutes, adds more value
- **Voicemail script** (under 30 words) — if they don't answer a call within 1 hour

Rules for ALL messages:
- Reference how they found me or what they were looking at — no generic "thanks for reaching out"
- Ask exactly ONE question to start a conversation (not "when can we meet?")
- Sound human and curious, not scripted or desperate
- The text message must be sendable in under 10 seconds (keep it short)

After the templates, give me a **Speed-to-Lead Cheat Sheet**:
| Lead Source | Response #1 (text) | Timing | Response #2 (email) | Timing | Response #3 (call) | Timing |

Do NOT write messages that sound like auto-responders. These should feel like a real person typed them quickly.
Do NOT use "I'd love to help you!" or "Don't hesitate to reach out!" — ban these phrases.
Do NOT front-load a sales pitch. The first message's only job is to start a conversation.
quickStart: Write instant-response templates for 7 lead sources (Zillow, website, Facebook ad, social DM, open house, referral, Google ad). For each: a text under 40 words, a follow-up email under 100 words, and a voicemail under 30 words. Make them sound human, not automated. I'm [NAME] in [MARKET].

title: Lead Magnet Promotion Plan
bestFor: ** Agents who built a lead magnet but nobody downloads it
prompt:
You are a lead magnet promotion strategist for real estate.

My lead magnet: [TITLE AND FORMAT]
My market: [CITY/AREA]
My platforms: [LIST — website, Instagram, Facebook, email list, etc.]
My email list size: [NUMBER]
My social media following: [APPROXIMATE TOTAL]

Create a 30-day promotion plan to maximize downloads. Give me the exact content for each promotional action:

| Day | Platform | Action | Content (write it out) |

Include at minimum:
- 6 social media posts promoting the lead magnet (write all captions, under 80 words each)
- 2 emails to my existing list (write both, under 120 words each)
- 3 story sequences (what text goes on each slide)
- 2 short-form video concepts (hook + CTA, under 50 words each)
- 1 Facebook/Instagram ad concept (copy under 100 words)
- Website placement recommendations (where to put the CTA)

Each promotional piece should use a different angle — don't say "download my free guide" 14 times in 14 different ways.

ANGLE EXAMPLES:
- "I just finished writing this — here's one thing I learned..."
- Share a stat from the guide, then offer the full thing
- "I get this question 5x a week. I wrote the answer down."

Do NOT suggest promoting on platforms I don't use.
Do NOT front-load everything into Day 1. Spread it out.
Do NOT write promotions that feel like ads. They should feel like value previews.
quickStart: Create a 30-day promotion plan for my lead magnet "[TITLE]." Give me the exact copy for 6 social posts, 2 emails, 3 story sequences, and 1 ad — each using a different angle. I'm on [PLATFORMS].

title: Email List Building Tactics
bestFor: ** Agents with fewer than 500 email subscribers
prompt:
You are an email list growth specialist for real estate.

My current email list: [NUMBER] contacts
My market: [CITY/AREA]
My website: [YES/NO and monthly visitors if yes]
My social media following: [APPROXIMATE TOTAL]
My in-person activities: [e.g., "open houses 2x/month," "networking events weekly," "none"]

Give me 10 specific tactics to grow my email list by 300+ subscribers in 90 days.

For each tactic:
| # | Tactic | Where (online/offline) | Effort (Low/Med/High) | Expected Subscribers in 90 Days | The Exact Setup (3 sentences max) |

Include at least:
- 3 online tactics
- 3 offline/in-person tactics
- 2 content-based tactics
- 2 partnership-based tactics

For my top 3 tactics (by effort-to-result ratio), write the exact copy/script I need:
- If it's a sign-up CTA: write the text
- If it's something I say in person: write the script (under 30 words)
- If it's a social media post: write the caption

Do NOT suggest "add a newsletter signup to your website" without telling me where, what the CTA says, and what they get for signing up.
Do NOT recommend buying email lists. Ever.
Do NOT count CRM contacts who never opted in as "email subscribers."
quickStart: Give me 10 tactics to add 300 email subscribers in 90 days. I'm a [MARKET] agent with [X] current subscribers and [describe your platforms/activities]. Write the exact CTA or script for the top 3 tactics.

title: Welcome Email Sequence Writer
bestFor: ** Agents who collect emails but never email those contacts
prompt:
You are an email marketing specialist for real estate. Write a 5-email welcome sequence for new subscribers.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My niche: [DESCRIBE]
How most people join my list: [e.g., "downloaded buyer guide" or "signed up for market updates" or "open house sign-in"]
My name: [YOUR NAME]

Write all 5 emails:

**Email 1 (send immediately):**
Subject line:
Preview text (under 50 characters):
Body (under 120 words):
Goal: Deliver what they signed up for + introduce myself in ONE sentence.

**Email 2 (Day 2):**
Subject line:
Body (under 100 words):
Goal: Share one valuable insight about [MARKET] — prove my expertise.

**Email 3 (Day 5):**
Subject line:
Body (under 100 words):
Goal: Tell a brief client success story — build trust.

**Email 4 (Day 8):**
Subject line:
Body (under 100 words):
Goal: Educate on one common misconception — position as advisor.

**Email 5 (Day 12):**
Subject line:
Body (under 120 words):
Goal: Soft CTA — invite a conversation, not a commitment.

TONE:
DO: Write like a knowledgeable friend sending a helpful email.
DON'T: Write like a corporation sending a marketing blast.

Do NOT use "Hi [FIRST NAME]!" as the opening of every email. Vary it.
Do NOT include more than ONE link per email. Focus.
Do NOT make Email 5's CTA "schedule a consultation." Make it softer: "reply to this email with your biggest question about [MARKET]."
quickStart: Write a 5-email welcome sequence for new subscribers. I'm [NAME] in [MARKET]. Emails at Day 0, 2, 5, 8, 12. Each under 120 words. Progression: deliver value → share expertise → client story → educate → soft CTA.

title: Weekly Market Update Email Template
bestFor: ** Agents who want a repeatable weekly email their audience looks forward to
prompt:
You are a real estate email content writer. Create a reusable weekly market update email template.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My list size: approximately [NUMBER]
My tone: [e.g., "data-driven and direct" or "friendly and conversational" or "fun with personality"]

Design the template with these sections (I'll swap in new data each week):

**Subject line formula:** Give me 5 formulas with examples:
| Formula | Example |
(e.g., Formula: "[Neighborhood] + surprising stat" → "Lincoln Park: 23% of homes sold over asking last week")

**Email structure:**

1. **Opening hook** (1-2 sentences, template with blanks I fill in):
"[Template: This week in [MARKET], [surprising thing happened]. Here's what it means for [buyers/sellers].]"

2. **The Numbers** (table format):
| Metric | This Week | Last Week | Trend |
[4 rows for key metrics]

3. **What It Means** (2-3 sentences — template language):
"[For buyers, this means ___. For sellers, this means ___.  My take: ___.]"

4. **Hot Listing or Neighborhood Spotlight** (3 sentences max):
Template for featuring one listing or area.

5. **CTA** (1 sentence — rotate between these 4 weekly):
Week 1: Question prompt ("Reply with your biggest question about ___")
Week 2: Lead magnet push ("Grab my free ___")
Week 3: Social follow ("See my latest [market/neighborhood] video")
Week 4: Direct offer ("Book a 15-minute market chat")

Keep the entire email under 250 words. Write one COMPLETE example email using sample data so I can see the template in action.

Do NOT design an email that takes more than 20 minutes to customize each week.
Do NOT include more than one image. Text-based emails get better deliverability.
Do NOT use "Happy [Day]!" as the opening. Start with the insight.
quickStart: Create a weekly market update email template I can fill in with new data in 15 minutes. Include: subject line formula, data table, "what it means" section, and rotating CTA. Write one complete example. Under 250 words per email.

title: Re-Engagement Email for Cold List
bestFor: ** Agents with email contacts who haven't heard from them in 6+ months
prompt:
You are an email reactivation specialist for real estate.

My situation: I have [NUMBER] email contacts I haven't emailed in [TIMEFRAME].
My market: [CITY/AREA]
These contacts are mostly: [e.g., "old leads who never bought," "past open house visitors," "past clients I lost touch with"]

Write a 3-email re-engagement sequence:

**Email 1 — "The Reconnection" (Day 1):**
Subject line (3 options):
Body (under 100 words):
Goal: Re-introduce myself without apologizing for going silent. Lead with value.

**Email 2 — "The Value Bomb" (Day 4):**
Subject line (3 options):
Body (under 100 words):
Goal: Share something genuinely useful about the [MARKET] market right now. Make them glad they opened.

**Email 3 — "The Clean Break" (Day 8):**
Subject line (3 options):
Body (under 80 words):
Goal: Ask if they want to keep hearing from me or be removed. This counterintuitive approach gets the highest engagement.

For Email 3, use this proven formula:
- Acknowledge it's been a while
- Offer clear value for staying
- Make it easy to unsubscribe (one click)
- The PS line that often converts: "P.S. If you're even slightly curious about what your home is worth now vs. [timeframe] ago, just reply 'VALUE' and I'll send you a quick update."

Do NOT apologize in any email. "Sorry I've been quiet" is weak. Lead with confidence and value.
Do NOT send all 3 on the same day or consecutive days. Space them out.
Do NOT be afraid of unsubscribes. A smaller engaged list beats a large dead one.
quickStart: Write a 3-email re-engagement sequence for [NUMBER] contacts I haven't emailed in [TIMEFRAME]. Email 1: reconnect. Email 2: value bomb. Email 3: stay or go? Each under 100 words. No apologies — lead with value.

title: Database Audit & Segmentation Plan
bestFor: ** Agents with 200+ contacts in a CRM they never organize
prompt:
You are a CRM and database specialist for real estate.

My CRM: [NAME — e.g., Follow Up Boss, KVCore, LionDesk, spreadsheet]
Total contacts: [NUMBER]
Breakdown (approximate): [X] past clients, [X] sphere of influence, [X] old leads, [X] other/unknown
Data quality: [GOOD / FAIR / POOR — e.g., "most have emails, many missing phone numbers"]

Create my database segmentation and cleanup plan:

1. **Tag taxonomy** — the exact tags/labels I should create:

| Tag Category | Tags | Definition |
|--------------|------|------------|

Include tags for: relationship type, lead temperature, transaction history, preferred communication channel, and lead source.

2. **Cleanup checklist** (in priority order):
- Step 1: [action] — estimated time: [X hours]
- Step 2-7: [continue]

3. **Segmentation rules** — after cleanup, how to sort contacts into communication tiers:

| Tier | Who Goes Here | How Often I Contact Them | Communication Type |
|------|---------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| A — VIP | | | |
| B — Active | | | |
| C — Nurture | | | |
| D — Archive | | | |

4. **"Quick wins" list** — 5 filters I can run RIGHT NOW to find contacts likely to transact in the next 12 months:
For each: the filter criteria, why these contacts are high-potential, and the action to take.

Do NOT suggest a CRM migration. Work with what I have.
Do NOT create more than 20 total tags. Too many tags = nobody uses them.
Do NOT skip the "archive" tier. Some contacts should be removed.
quickStart: Create a database segmentation plan for my [CRM] with [NUMBER] contacts. Give me: a tag taxonomy (under 20 tags), cleanup checklist, 4-tier communication plan, and 5 filters to find contacts most likely to transact soon.

title: Past Client Reactivation Campaign
bestFor: ** Agents with 50+ past clients they've lost touch with
prompt:
You are a real estate relationship reactivation specialist.

I have [NUMBER] past clients I haven't contacted in [TIMEFRAME].
My market: [CITY/AREA]
Most of these clients bought/sold [TIMEFRAME] ago.
Average home price when they transacted: approximately $[AMOUNT]

Write a 5-touch reactivation campaign:

**Touch 1 — Personal text message (Day 1):**
"[Write exact text, under 160 characters. Must feel personal, not mass-sent. Reference homeownership.]"

**Touch 2 — Email with value (Day 3):**
Subject line:
Body (under 100 words): Include a market insight relevant to homeowners in my area. End with a question.

**Touch 3 — Handwritten note talking points (Day 7):**
Write 3 sentences I should handwrite on a notecard. Under 40 words total.

**Touch 4 — Phone call script (Day 14):**
Write the complete script (under 100 words). Include: opening, value statement, and one natural transition to "do you know anyone who..."
Handle if they don't answer: voicemail script (under 25 words).

**Touch 5 — Social media engagement (Day 14-21):**
Describe 3 actions: engage with their posts, share something relevant, send a DM. Write the DM (under 30 words).

TONE:
DO: "Hey [Name], I was thinking about you — saw a home on your street just sold for $[X]. Pretty wild what's happened with values in [neighborhood]. How are you loving the house?"
DON'T: "Hi [Name]! Just reaching out to see if you or anyone you know is looking to buy or sell!"

Do NOT make every touch a referral ask. Only Touch 4 should naturally surface referrals.
Do NOT send all touches via the same channel. Mix text, email, physical mail, phone, and social.
Do NOT send mass messages. Each touch should feel like it's only going to them.
quickStart: Write a 5-touch reactivation campaign for past clients I haven't contacted in [TIMEFRAME]. Mix: text, email, handwritten note, phone call, and social media. Only one touch should ask for referrals. Each must feel personal, not mass-sent.

title: Database "Hidden Opportunity" Finder
bestFor: ** Agents with 300+ contacts who want to mine their database for transactions
prompt:
You are a real estate database analyst. Help me identify hidden transaction opportunities in my existing database.

My database size: [NUMBER] contacts
My market: [CITY/AREA]
Average local homeowner tenure before selling: [X years, or "I don't know"]
Local annual turnover rate: [X%, or "I don't know"]

Give me 8 "opportunity filters" — specific criteria I can search in my CRM to find contacts most likely to transact soon. For each:

| # | Filter Name | CRM Search Criteria | Why These Contacts Are Likely to Transact | Outreach Message (under 50 words) |

Filters should include:
1. Past clients who bought 5+ years ago (equity play)
2. Contacts in high-appreciation ZIP codes
3. Leads who inquired but never transacted (9-18 months ago)
4. Empty nesters (purchased 4+ bed homes 15+ years ago)
5. Contacts who engaged with recent emails but didn't respond
6-8. Three more based on your expertise

For filters 1, 3, and 4, write the complete outreach message (text or email) I'd send.

Then give me the math:
"If your database has [X] contacts, statistically [Y] of them will transact in the next 12 months (based on [Z]% national move rate). If you capture [A]% of those, that's [B] transactions worth approximately $[C] in GCI."

Do NOT suggest opportunity filters that require data I'm unlikely to have (e.g., "filter by household income").
Do NOT make the outreach messages feel like a mass campaign. They should feel like personal check-ins.
Do NOT forget the math — that's what motivates me to actually do this.
quickStart: Give me 8 CRM filters to find hidden transaction opportunities in my database of [NUMBER] contacts. For each: the search criteria, why they're likely to transact, and a 50-word outreach message. Show me the math on potential GCI.

title: Annual Database "Purge" Email
bestFor: ** Agents with a large, unresponsive email list
prompt:
You are an email list management specialist. Write my annual database purge email — the "should I stay or should I go" message.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My name: [YOUR NAME]
My list size: [NUMBER]
My open rate: approximately [PERCENTAGE]%

Write the email:

**Subject line (3 options — each must create curiosity):**
1.
2.
3.

**Body (under 120 words):**
- Opening: Acknowledge that their inbox is crowded
- The offer: Here's what they GET by staying (be specific — market updates, early listing access, etc.)
- The out: Make it easy and guilt-free to unsubscribe
- The hook: A P.S. line that re-engages the curious

**The P.S. line is the most important part.** It should offer something specific enough that fence-sitters click. Examples:
- "P.S. Homes in [neighborhood] just hit a 3-year price record. Reply 'UPDATE' for your home's current estimated value."
- "P.S. I'm putting together a list of off-market properties for Q2. Reply 'LIST' to get on it."

Write 3 P.S. options.

Then give me the decision framework:
- If someone opens but doesn't click → [what to do]
- If someone clicks the stay link → [what to do]
- If someone unsubscribes → [what to do]
- If someone doesn't open → [what to do after 7 days]

Do NOT be needy. "We'd hate to see you go!" is weak.
Do NOT threaten removal. Offer it as a favor.
Do NOT send this to past clients. Only to leads and loose contacts.
quickStart: Write a "should I stay or should I go" database purge email. Under 120 words. 3 subject lines, 3 P.S. hooks that re-engage the curious. Tell me what to do with openers, clickers, unsubscribers, and non-openers.

title: First-Time Buyer Lead Generation Playbook
bestFor: ** Agents targeting the first-time buyer market
prompt:
You are a first-time homebuyer marketing specialist.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
Median home price: $[AMOUNT]
Typical first-time buyer profile here: [AGE, INCOME, CURRENT SITUATION]
My current first-time buyer content: [DESCRIBE or "none"]

Give me a complete first-time buyer lead generation playbook:

1. **5 content pieces that attract first-time buyers** (titles, format, and the hook for each):
| # | Title | Format (Blog/Video/Carousel/Guide) | Hook (first line) | Lead Capture CTA |

2. **3 partnership opportunities** specifically for first-time buyer leads:
| Partner Type | What I Offer Them | What They Offer Me | Outreach Message (under 50 words) |

3. **5 social media post ideas** that make renters think about buying:
For each, write the complete caption (under 80 words) and specify the format.

4. **1 lead magnet concept** designed specifically for first-time buyers:
- Title:
- Format:
- 5 things it covers:
- Landing page headline:

TONE for first-time buyer content:
DO: "Nobody explained this to me when I bought my first place either. Here's what you actually need to know."
DON'T: "Congratulations on taking the first step toward your homeownership journey!"

Do NOT talk down to first-time buyers. They're making the biggest purchase of their life — respect that.
Do NOT use terms without explaining them (escrow, contingency, DTI) in any content piece.
Do NOT assume all first-time buyers are young. Many are 35-50.
quickStart: Give me a first-time buyer lead gen playbook for [MARKET]: 5 content pieces with hooks, 3 partnership ideas with outreach messages, 5 social posts (full captions), and 1 lead magnet concept.

title: Luxury Market Lead Generation Strategy
bestFor: ** Agents breaking into or growing in the luxury market ($1M+)
prompt:
You are a luxury real estate marketing strategist.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
Luxury threshold in my market: $[MINIMUM PRICE]+
My current luxury experience: [DESCRIBE]
My current HNWI (high-net-worth individual) contacts: [NUMBER]
My biggest challenge: [DESCRIBE]

Give me a luxury lead generation strategy:

1. **5 places where HNWI prospects can be found** (specific, not generic):
| # | Where | How to Access | Approach (under 30 words) | Expected Timeline to Generate Leads |

NOT: "Join a country club." YES: "Volunteer for the fundraising committee at [type of charity gala]. Position: auction coordinator — you'll meet every major donor."

2. **3 luxury content pieces** that attract high-end clients:
| # | Title | Format | Why It Attracts Wealth | Distribution Channel |

3. **Luxury brand positioning checklist** — 10 specific things to change about my marketing to signal "luxury":
| # | Element | What to Change | Example |

4. **5 referral partner types** specific to luxury transactions:
| Partner Type | Why They Have Luxury Referrals | My Opening Line (under 25 words) |

TONE for luxury marketing:
DO: Understated confidence. Data-driven. Exclusive without being exclusionary.
DON'T: "LUXURY" in all caps. Gold fonts. "Prestige" and "opulent." Trying too hard signals you're not luxury.

Do NOT suggest I "fake it till I make it" with luxury. Give me authentic ways to build credibility.
Do NOT recommend expensive branding exercises. Focus on actions that generate leads directly.
Do NOT assume luxury clients use social media the same way mainstream clients do.
quickStart: I'm breaking into luxury real estate ($[X]+) in [MARKET]. Give me: 5 specific places to find HNWI prospects, 3 luxury content ideas, a 10-point luxury branding checklist, and 5 referral partner types.

title: Investor Lead Generation System
bestFor: ** Agents wanting to attract real estate investors
prompt:
You are a real estate investment marketing specialist.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
Types of investors I want to attract: [e.g., "buy-and-hold rental investors," "house flippers," "1031 exchange buyers," "small multifamily buyers"]
My investment knowledge level: [BEGINNER / INTERMEDIATE / EXPERT]
Current investor clients: [NUMBER]

Create an investor lead generation system:

1. **5 "investor magnet" content pieces** — content that attracts investors, not regular buyers:
| # | Title | Why Investors Care | Format | Where to Distribute |

2. **The Investor Deal Alert Template** — write the email template for sending investment opportunities (under 150 words). Include:
- Subject line formula:
- Property snapshot format (what data points investors need):
- The ROI/return presentation:
- CTA:

3. **3 platforms/communities where investors gather** (beyond BiggerPockets):
| # | Platform | How to Provide Value There | Lead Gen Strategy |

4. **Investment-focused social media post** — write 3 complete posts that would attract investors:
Each under 80 words. Include specific numbers/returns, not vague "great investment opportunity" language.

5. **1031 exchange lead capture strategy** in 5 bullet points — how to position myself as the agent investors call when they're exchanging.

INVESTOR TONE:
DO: Lead with numbers. Cap rate, cash-on-cash return, price-per-door, rent-to-price ratio.
DON'T: "Beautiful investment opportunity in a desirable neighborhood!"

Do NOT write for investors the way you'd write for homebuyers. Investors buy spreadsheets, not kitchens.
Do NOT suggest I become a financial advisor. Stay in my lane but speak their language.
Do NOT ignore the disclaimer: "Not financial advice" should be noted where appropriate.
quickStart: Create an investor lead gen system for [MARKET]: 5 content pieces that attract investors, a deal alert email template, 3 platforms where investors gather, and 3 investment-focused social posts with real numbers.

title: Expired Listing Prospecting System
bestFor: ** Agents willing to prospect expired listings for seller leads
prompt:
You are an expired listing prospecting specialist.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My current prospecting: [DESCRIBE frequency and approach, or "none"]
My biggest challenge: [DESCRIBE]

Build my expired listing prospecting system:

1. **Pre-call research prompt** — write the prompt I paste into AI before calling each expired listing:
"Here's the listing data for [ADDRESS]: [paste MLS data]. In 3 bullet points, tell me why this home likely didn't sell and what I'd do differently."

2. **Phone script** (complete, word-for-word, under 200 words):
- Opening (first 10 seconds — NOT "Hi, I noticed your listing expired"):
- 3 qualifying questions disguised as empathy:
- Value statement (15 seconds):
- Appointment close:
- If they say no: graceful exit + permission to follow up

3. **Voicemail script** (under 30 seconds / 70 words):

4. **Follow-up text** (sent 2 minutes after voicemail, under 160 characters):

5. **Follow-up email** (sent same day if no response, under 100 words):
Subject line:
Body with one specific insight about their listing.

6. **Objection responses** for the top 5 expired listing objections:
| Objection | Response (under 50 words) |

Do NOT start the phone script with "I'm calling about your home that was listed..." — they've heard that 20 times today.
Do NOT be aggressive. Empathy and expertise win expired listings, not pressure.
Do NOT include a "motivational mindset" section. Just give me the scripts.

See Chapter 3 for the long-term follow-up nurture sequence after initial contact.
quickStart: Write an expired listing prospecting system: pre-call research AI prompt, phone script (under 200 words), voicemail (under 70 words), follow-up text (under 160 chars), follow-up email (under 100 words), and responses to the top 5 objections.

title: FSBO Conversion Approach System
bestFor: ** Agents targeting For Sale By Owner listings
prompt:
You are a FSBO conversion specialist.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
Average FSBO discount vs. agent-assisted sale in my area: [X% or "I don't know"]
My approach to FSBOs so far: [DESCRIBE or "haven't tried"]

Build my FSBO approach system:

1. **FSBO identification checklist** — 5 places to find FSBOs daily:
| # | Source | How to Search | Expected FSBOs/Week |

2. **"Value-first" initial contact script** (phone, under 150 words):
The key: Do NOT pitch listing services on the first call. Offer something free and useful first.
- Opening:
- Free offer (e.g., market data, comp analysis, staging tips):
- Close for an appointment to deliver the free item IN PERSON:

3. **FSBO-specific ad** — write a Facebook/Instagram ad targeting FSBOs (under 100 words):
Angle: "I help FSBOs sell for more — even if you don't list with me."

4. **The "FSBO service menu"** — 5 services I can offer FSBOs at no charge that demonstrate my value:
| # | Service | Why It's Valuable | How It Leads to a Listing |

5. **Top 5 FSBO objections with responses:**
| Objection | Psychology Behind It | Response (under 50 words) |

FSBO TONE:
DO: Position as an ally, not a predator. "I respect that you're doing this yourself. Here's how I can help, with no strings."
DON'T: "Studies show FSBOs sell for less." (They've heard this. It sounds self-serving.)

Do NOT assume FSBOs are naive. Many are smart, motivated people.
Do NOT pitch commission savings on the first contact. Build value first.
Do NOT be pushy about listing agreements. The goal of call #1 is an in-person meeting, not a signature.
quickStart: Write a FSBO approach system: 5 identification sources, a value-first phone script (under 150 words), a Facebook ad targeting FSBOs (under 100 words), 5 free services I can offer, and responses to the top 5 FSBO objections.

title: Direct Mail Campaign Writer (12-Month)
bestFor: ** Agents farming a geographic area
prompt:
You are a direct mail specialist for real estate geographic farming.

My farm area: [NEIGHBORHOOD/AREA]
Number of homes: [NUMBER]
Average home price: $[AMOUNT]
My monthly direct mail budget: $[AMOUNT]
My brand/personality: [DESCRIBE in one sentence]

Design 12 months of direct mail pieces. For each month:

| Month | Format | Topic/Theme | Complete Headline | Complete Back Copy (under 75 words) | CTA | Tracking Method |

Formats should vary: postcards (6), letters (2), market reports (2), specialty pieces (2).

Requirements:
- Each piece must have ONE clear CTA (not 3 different things to do)
- Copy must be written out completely — not "include market stats" but the actual text
- Every piece should include a URL or QR code I can track
- At least 4 pieces should be designed to generate seller leads
- At least 2 pieces should position me as the neighborhood expert
- At least 2 pieces should include actual sold data (with placeholders like "$[SOLD PRICE]")

TONE:
DO: Feel like a neighbor sharing useful information.
DON'T: Feel like an advertisement from someone trying to get a listing.

Do NOT write "Just Listed / Just Sold" for more than 3 of the 12 pieces. That's lazy farming.
Do NOT include my headshot description. I'll handle the design.
Do NOT suggest expensive "lumpy mail" or dimensional mailers. Stick to flat mail on my budget.
quickStart: Write 12 months of direct mail pieces for farming [NUMBER] homes in [AREA]. Include the complete copy for each piece (under 75 words each). Vary formats: postcards, letters, market reports. Each piece needs one clear CTA.

title: Door-Knocking Scripts
bestFor: ** Agents willing to door-knock in their farm area
prompt:
You are a real estate door-knocking script writer.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My farm area: [NEIGHBORHOOD]
My reason for knocking today: [e.g., "just listed a home nearby," "just sold a home nearby," "introducing myself to the neighborhood," "delivering a market update," "promoting an open house"]

Write 5 door-knocking scripts for 5 different scenarios. Each script must:
- Be under 60 words (you have 20-30 seconds before they want to close the door)
- Start with my reason for being there (not "Hi, I'm a real estate agent")
- Include one question that gets them talking
- End with a leave-behind offer

**Script 1: Just Listed Nearby**
"[Complete script]"
Leave-behind: [what I hand them]

**Script 2: Just Sold Nearby**
"[Complete script]"
Leave-behind:

**Script 3: First Introduction to the Area**
"[Complete script]"
Leave-behind:

**Script 4: Market Update Delivery**
"[Complete script]"
Leave-behind:

**Script 5: Nobody Home — Door Hanger Message**
"[Write the text for a door hanger, under 40 words, with a CTA]"

Then write responses for these 3 common reactions:
- "I'm not interested": [response, under 20 words]
- "We're not planning to move": [response, under 25 words]
- "We already have an agent": [response, under 20 words]

Do NOT make these scripts sound rehearsed. They should feel conversational.
Do NOT open with a question. Open with why you're there.
Do NOT ask "Are you thinking of selling?" at the door. Too aggressive for a first meeting.
quickStart: Write 5 door-knocking scripts (under 60 words each) for: just listed nearby, just sold nearby, first introduction, market update, and a door hanger for when nobody's home. Include responses to "not interested," "not moving," and "have an agent."

title: Community Event Lead Generation Plan
bestFor: ** Agents who want to generate leads through in-person events (not open houses)
prompt:
You are a community event marketing planner for real estate.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My farm area: [NEIGHBORHOOD]
My budget per event: $[AMOUNT]
My personality/brand: [DESCRIBE]
Events I've hosted before: [DESCRIBE or "none"]

Design 4 community events I can host this year (one per quarter). For each:

**Event [#]: [Name]**
- **Concept:** (2 sentences)
- **Target attendee:** (who comes to this?)
- **Budget estimate:** $[range]
- **Lead capture method:** (how I collect contact info without being salesy)
- **Promotional copy:** (write a social media post promoting it, under 80 words)
- **My role during the event:** (what I do/say to be helpful, not "pitch" real estate)
- **Follow-up message:** (write the text I send attendees within 24 hours, under 50 words)

Events should be:
- Q1: [Lower budget, indoor, relationship-building]
- Q2: [Moderate budget, outdoor possible, family-friendly]
- Q3: [Higher budget, signature event, maximum attendance]
- Q4: [Appreciation/holiday theme, warmth-building]

Do NOT suggest events that require more than 4 hours of my time to plan and execute.
Do NOT suggest a "homebuyer seminar" — those attract agents, not clients.
Do NOT make every event about real estate. The best lead gen events have nothing to do with houses.

See Chapter 4 for open house-specific event strategies.
See Chapter 5 for networking event strategies.
quickStart: Design 4 quarterly community events for a real estate agent in [AREA] with $[BUDGET] per event. Each needs: the concept, lead capture method, promotional social post (under 80 words), and a 24-hour follow-up text (under 50 words).

title: Lead Scoring Framework Builder
bestFor: ** Agents with 20+ incoming leads per month who need to prioritize
prompt:
You are a real estate lead scoring specialist.

My lead sources: [LIST ALL SOURCES]
Monthly lead volume: approximately [NUMBER]
My biggest problem: [e.g., "I spend equal time on all leads and close very few" or "I can't tell hot leads from cold ones"]

Build a lead scoring framework I can actually use:

1. **Scoring criteria table:**

| Criteria | Points | Examples |
|----------|--------|---------|
| Lead source | +1 to +5 | Referral = +5, Zillow = +3, Cold social = +1 |
| Timeline | +1 to +5 | [fill in] |
| Pre-approval status | +1 to +5 | [fill in] |
| Engagement level | +1 to +5 | [fill in] |
| Motivation indicator | +1 to +5 | [fill in] |

Include 8-10 scoring criteria total.

2. **Score ranges and actions:**

| Score | Label | Response Time | Action |
|-------|-------|---------------|--------|
| 20-25 | Hot | Within 5 min | [specific action] |
| 13-19 | Warm | Within 2 hours | [specific action] |
| 7-12 | Cool | Within 24 hours | [specific action] |
| 1-6 | Cold | Within 48 hours | [specific action] |

3. **5 qualifying questions** I can ask in the first conversation that help me score accurately:
For each question, tell me what their answer reveals and how to score it.

Do NOT make this so complex that I need software to calculate it. Mental math or a simple spreadsheet.
Do NOT include criteria I can't assess in the first 48 hours of having the lead.
Do NOT create a scoring system with more than 25 total possible points. Keep it simple.

See Chapter 3 for what to do with each lead tier after scoring.
quickStart: Build a lead scoring system with 8-10 criteria, each worth 1-5 points. Give me score ranges (Hot/Warm/Cool/Cold) with response time rules and specific actions for each tier. Max 25 total points. Keep it simple enough for mental math.

title: Lead Qualification Call Script
bestFor: ** Agents who struggle to determine if a lead is worth their time
prompt:
You are a real estate lead qualification specialist.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
The lead I'm calling: [SOURCE — e.g., "website registration," "Facebook ad lead," "open house sign-in"]
What I know about them: [WHATEVER INFO I HAVE]

Write a qualification call script (under 150 words spoken) that determines within 5 minutes:

1. **Are they motivated?** (timeline question)
2. **Are they able?** (financial readiness question)
3. **Am I the right agent?** (are they already working with someone?)
4. **What's the next step?** (appointment or nurture?)

Format:

**Opening (under 20 words):**
"[Exact words — reference how they came to me]"

**Question 1 — Timeline:**
"[Question]"
- If answer signals urgency → [continue to Q2]
- If answer signals "just looking" → [what to say]

**Question 2 — Financial readiness:**
"[Question — must not feel invasive]"

**Question 3 — Agent status:**
"[Question — NOT 'Are you working with an agent?' — find a better way to ask]"

**Question 4 — Close for next step:**
- Hot lead close: "[Exact words to book an appointment]"
- Warm lead close: "[Exact words to set a follow-up]"
- Cold lead close: "[Exact words to exit gracefully and add to nurture]"

TONE:
DO: Sound like a doctor doing a brief intake — professional, efficient, caring.
DON'T: Sound like a telemarketer running through a checklist.

Do NOT ask more than 4 questions. This is qualification, not interrogation.
Do NOT ask "What's your budget?" directly. Ask it indirectly.
Do NOT skip the "cold lead close." Most leads are cold — handle it well and they'll come back.

See Chapter 3 for follow-up sequences after qualification.
quickStart: Write a 5-minute lead qualification call script with 4 questions covering: timeline, financial readiness, agent status, and next step. Include what to say for hot, warm, and cold outcomes. Lead source: [SOURCE]. Under 150 words spoken.

title: Same-Day Showing Debrief Script
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents after any showing day
prompt:
You are a buyer's agent who excels at post-showing conversations. Write a natural, conversational debrief script I can use in the car or at coffee immediately after showings.

Context: I just showed [NUMBER] properties to [CLIENT DESCRIPTION -- e.g., "a first-time buyer couple in their early 30s"]. Here's what happened at each property: [BRIEF REACTIONS].

Write a debrief conversation flow that includes:
1. One opening question that invites honest reflection (not "What did you think?")
2. A simple comparison framework -- 3 questions that help them rank what they saw
3. Two "hidden needs" questions that reveal what they learned about their own priorities
4. A next-steps suggestion for each scenario: they loved one, they liked several, they liked none

Format: Write it as a natural dialogue outline with my lines in bold and brief stage directions in italics.

Keep the total script under 400 words. The tone should feel like a trusted advisor chatting naturally -- not an interview.

Do NOT use phrases like "So what are your thoughts?" or "On a scale of 1 to 10."
Do NOT push toward an offer unless the client's energy clearly warrants it.
Do NOT script their responses -- just write my side with transition notes.

Example of the tone I want:
DO: "Okay, real talk -- which kitchen could you actually see yourself making Sunday pancakes in?"
DON'T: "Based on the properties we viewed today, which one best aligns with your stated criteria?"
quickStart: Write a casual, 5-question post-showing debrief script for my buyer client [CLIENT DESCRIPTION] who just saw [NUMBER] homes. Help them compare properties and figure out next steps without feeling pressured. Keep it conversational, under 300 words.

title: 2-Hour Post-Showing Follow-Up Email
bestFor: ** Sending within 2 hours of any showing
prompt:
You are a buyer's agent writing a follow-up email within 2 hours of showing properties. Write one personalized email.

Context:
- Client: [NAME, DESCRIPTION]
- Properties shown: [LIST WITH ADDRESSES]
- Their reactions: [WHAT THEY SAID/FELT ABOUT EACH]
- Their search criteria: [BUDGET, MUST-HAVES, TIMELINE]

Write the email with these sections:
1. A warm opening that references something specific they said or did during the showings (1-2 sentences)
2. A brief property recap -- for each home, 2-3 bullet points capturing THEIR perspective (what they liked and didn't), not generic listing features
3. A next-step CTA tailored to their energy level:
   - If excited about one property: suggest discussing next steps
   - If thoughtful/undecided: give them space to reflect
   - If disappointed: signal you have new ideas

Keep the email under 200 words. Subject line should reference something personal from the showing, not be generic.

Do NOT use "Just following up" or "Hope you're doing well."
Do NOT list property features they can find on Zillow -- reference their personal reactions.
Do NOT include a CTA for every property -- focus on what matters most to them.

Tone example:
DO: "That backyard on Pine Street -- I saw your face light up when you spotted the fire pit area."
DON'T: "Thank you for your time today. Below please find a summary of the properties we toured."
quickStart: Write a short, warm follow-up email to [CLIENT NAME] who I showed [NUMBER] homes to today. Reference their specific reactions: [REACTIONS]. Under 200 words, personal tone, one clear next step.

title: "They Loved It" Same-Evening Follow-Up Text
bestFor: ** When a buyer clearly connected with a property
prompt:
You are a buyer's agent. My client [CLIENT NAME/DESCRIPTION] clearly loved the property at [ADDRESS] during today's showing. Signs they loved it: [DESCRIBE -- e.g., "they measured the nursery, asked about the school district twice, lingered in the backyard"].

Write 3 text message options I can send this evening (6-8 PM) that:
1. Acknowledge their excitement without being pushy
2. Offer one relevant piece of information (market context, not pressure tactics)
3. End with a soft next step

Each text must be under 160 characters OR under 300 characters (provide both lengths for each option).

Do NOT use urgency phrases like "won't last long" or "multiple offers expected" unless I specifically tell you that's true.
Do NOT sound like a salesperson closing a deal -- sound like a friend who's genuinely excited for them.
Do NOT ask "Are you ready to make an offer?" directly.

Tone example:
DO: "Still thinking about that kitchen on Elm? I pulled up the tax history -- it's interesting. Call me when you're free."
DON'T: "Hi [Name], I wanted to follow up on today's showing. The property at 123 Elm St. is competitively priced and I recommend we act quickly."
quickStart: Write 3 casual evening text messages for my buyer who loved the home at [ADDRESS]. Excited but not pushy tone. Each under 160 characters.

title: "They Loved It" Next-Morning Momentum Text
bestFor: ** The morning after a great showing
prompt:
You are a buyer's agent. Yesterday my client [CLIENT NAME/DESCRIPTION] was very excited about [ADDRESS]. I sent an evening text (context: [WHAT I SENT]). It's now the next morning.

Write 3 options for a morning follow-up text (8-9 AM) that:
1. Gently checks if they're still feeling excited after sleeping on it
2. Provides one new, small piece of value (a detail they'd care about)
3. Makes it easy for them to respond with a short answer

Each text under 160 characters.

Do NOT repeat anything from last night's message.
Do NOT apply pressure -- let their enthusiasm do the work.
Do NOT send a novel. Morning texts should be light and quick.

Tone:
DO: "Morning! Random thought -- did you notice the built-in bookshelves in that second bedroom? Perfect for [their hobby]."
DON'T: "Good morning! Have you had a chance to think about the property? I'm available today if you'd like to discuss making an offer."
quickStart: Write a short, cheerful morning-after text for my buyer who loved [ADDRESS] yesterday. One small new detail, under 160 characters, easy to reply to.

title: "They're Undecided" 24-Hour Breathing Room Text
bestFor: ** When buyers are lukewarm or can't decide
prompt:
You are a buyer's agent. My client [CLIENT NAME/DESCRIPTION] saw [NUMBER] properties yesterday and is undecided. Their vibe was: [DESCRIBE -- e.g., "liked two but couldn't articulate why neither felt right" or "overwhelmed by options"].

Write 3 text message options for a 24-hour follow-up that:
1. Gives them explicit permission to take time and process
2. Shows empathy for how big the decision is
3. Plants one small seed of helpful perspective

Each text under 200 characters.

Do NOT ask them to rank properties or make a decision.
Do NOT send a list of pros and cons (save that for email).
Do NOT say "no rush" and then follow it with a question that creates urgency.

Tone:
DO: "Hey -- big decisions deserve a good night's sleep. No need to figure it all out right now. I'll be here when something clicks."
DON'T: "Hi [Name], I wanted to check in on your thoughts regarding yesterday's showings. Have you had a chance to narrow down your preferences?"
quickStart: Write a short, reassuring text for my undecided buyer [CLIENT NAME] 24 hours after showings. Give them space. Under 200 characters. Warm, zero pressure.

title: "They're Undecided" 48-Hour Value-Add Email
bestFor: ** Two days after showings when buyers need a new angle
prompt:
You are a buyer's agent. My client [CLIENT NAME/DESCRIPTION] is still undecided 48 hours after seeing [PROPERTIES]. Here's what I know about their hesitation: [DESCRIBE].

Write a short email (under 150 words) that offers a genuinely new perspective they haven't considered. Choose ONE of these angles based on what fits their situation:
- A neighborhood insight they might not know (ask me to fill in specifics)
- A market data point that reframes their thinking
- A creative suggestion (e.g., "What if we looked at [nearby area] where you'd get X for less?")
- A question that helps them articulate what's missing

The email should feel like a thoughtful friend sharing an idea -- not a salesperson pushing product.

Subject line: Something specific and intriguing, not "Checking in."

Do NOT recap the showings -- they remember.
Do NOT include more than one idea. One insight, clearly presented.
Do NOT end with "Let me know your thoughts" -- give a specific, low-effort CTA instead.

Tone:
DO: Subject: "A thought about that backyard situation." Body: Shares one insight, asks one specific question.
DON'T: Subject: "Following up on your property search." Body: Recaps everything, asks five questions.
quickStart: Write a 150-word email with one fresh perspective for my undecided buyer [NAME], 48 hours after showings. One new insight, one specific question. No recap.

title: "They Didn't Like Anything" Recovery Email
bestFor: ** When showings completely missed the mark
prompt:
You are a buyer's agent. My client [CLIENT NAME/DESCRIPTION] didn't connect with any of the [NUMBER] properties I showed them. Their reactions: [DESCRIBE]. I think the miss was because: [YOUR THEORY -- e.g., "I focused too much on budget and not enough on neighborhood feel"].

Write a recovery email (under 200 words) that:
1. Takes ownership honestly -- acknowledge the miss without over-apologizing (1-2 sentences)
2. Shows I learned something from the experience -- name what I now understand about what they want (2-3 sentences)
3. Presents a revised approach -- one specific change to the search strategy (2-3 sentences)
4. Ends with a CTA that re-engages them in the process

Subject line should be honest and forward-looking.

Do NOT blame the market, inventory, or the client's criteria.
Do NOT list 10 new properties to "make up for it" -- that overwhelms.
Do NOT be overly apologetic. Confidence and course-correction are more reassuring than apologies.

Tone:
DO: "Yesterday wasn't it -- and that's actually useful. Here's what I think I missed about what you're looking for..."
DON'T: "I sincerely apologize that the properties I selected did not meet your expectations. I assure you I will work harder to..."
quickStart: Write a 200-word recovery email for my buyer [NAME] after a bad showing day. Own the miss, show what I learned, propose one strategy change. Confident tone.

title: Post-Showing Criteria Refinement Questions
bestFor: ** After showings reveal a gap between stated and actual preferences
prompt:
You are a buyer's agent. After showing properties to [CLIENT DESCRIPTION], I've realized their stated criteria don't match what actually excites (or disappoints) them in person.

What they said they wanted: [STATED CRITERIA]
What they actually responded to: [OBSERVED REACTIONS]

Generate 10 questions I can ask in a phone call or coffee meeting that help them uncover their TRUE priorities. The questions should:
- Be open-ended and exploratory
- Reference specific moments from our showings ("Remember when you walked into that kitchen and immediately said...?")
- Help them distinguish between "nice to have" and "must have"
- Uncover emotional drivers, not just feature checklists

Format: Numbered list of 10 questions, each with a 1-sentence note explaining what the question is designed to uncover.

Do NOT ask "What are your deal-breakers?" (they already told me and it wasn't accurate).
Do NOT make them feel wrong about their original criteria -- frame it as discovery.
Do NOT include more than 2 questions about budget. Focus on lifestyle and feeling.
quickStart: Write 10 open-ended questions to help my buyer [DESCRIPTION] uncover their true home priorities after showings revealed their stated criteria don't match their gut reactions. Reference specific showing moments.

title: Post-Showing Competitive Intelligence Update
bestFor: ** When a property the buyer liked has real market activity
prompt:
You are a buyer's agent. My client [CLIENT NAME] is interested in [PROPERTY ADDRESS]. I need to share relevant market context to help them make an informed decision.

Current facts about this property:
- Days on market: [NUMBER]
- Price changes: [DESCRIBE]
- Known interest: [WHAT YOU KNOW -- e.g., "listing agent mentioned 2 other showings this week"]
- Comparable recent sales: [1-2 KEY COMPS]

Write a text message AND a short email (under 150 words) sharing this market intelligence.

The text should: share one key data point that matters most, under 160 characters.
The email should: present 3-4 market facts clearly, explain what they mean for the buyer's decision, and end with a specific question (not "Let me know").

Do NOT fabricate urgency -- only share verified facts.
Do NOT use fear-based language ("You might lose it!").
Do NOT make the decision for them -- present information and let them lead.

Tone:
DO: "Quick market note on Elm St -- two comparable homes on that block sold in under 10 days last month. Not saying rush, just want you to have the full picture."
DON'T: "URGENT: This property is generating significant interest and I strongly recommend we submit an offer immediately to avoid losing out."
quickStart: Write a factual, no-pressure text (160 chars) and email (150 words) sharing market data about [ADDRESS] with my interested buyer. Facts only, no manufactured urgency.

title: Post-Showing Financial Snapshot Email
bestFor: ** When a buyer likes a property but hasn't thought about the numbers
prompt:
You are a buyer's agent. My client [CLIENT NAME/DESCRIPTION] is interested in [PROPERTY ADDRESS, LIST PRICE]. Help me send a quick financial picture.

Client details:
- Estimated down payment: [AMOUNT OR PERCENTAGE]
- Approximate interest rate: [CURRENT RATE]
- Other monthly costs to include: [HOA, TAXES, INSURANCE ESTIMATES]

Write a short email (under 150 words) that presents the estimated monthly cost in a clear, simple format. Include:
1. A simple cost breakdown table (principal + interest, taxes, insurance, HOA, total)
2. One comparison point (e.g., vs. their current rent, or vs. a different property they're considering)
3. A note that these are estimates and a lender can provide exact numbers

Do NOT present this as financial advice.
Do NOT make the numbers feel scary -- context matters (compare to rent, show equity building).
Do NOT bury the total monthly number. Lead with it, then break it down.

Format the cost breakdown as a clean table.
quickStart: Write a 150-word email with a simple monthly cost breakdown table for [PROPERTY ADDRESS] at [PRICE] with [DOWN PAYMENT] down. Compare to [CURRENT RENT]. Note these are estimates.

title: Client Intelligence Profile Template
bestFor: ** Every new client relationship
prompt:
You are a client experience specialist. Create a "Client DNA" profile template I can fill in for each client to enable deeply personalized communication throughout our relationship.

Design the template with these categories:
1. **Personal Details** (family members, pets, hobbies, important dates)
2. **Communication Style** (preferred channel, best times, detail level, formality)
3. **Decision-Making Style** (fast/slow, data-driven/gut-feeling, who else influences decisions)
4. **Emotional Drivers** (what excites them about moving, what worries them, life changes motivating the move)
5. **Random Personal Notes** (favorite restaurant, team they root for, vacation they mentioned, book they're reading)

Format: A clean, fillable template with specific field names and example entries for each. Keep it to one page -- if it's too long, I won't use it.

Do NOT include fields I can get from MLS or my CRM's standard fields (address, email, phone).
Do NOT make it feel like a dossier -- it should feel like notes a thoughtful friend keeps.
Do NOT include more than 20 fields total. Constraint forces focus.
quickStart: Create a 20-field "Client DNA" profile template covering personal details, communication preferences, decision style, emotional drivers, and personal interests. Fillable format, one page.

title: Natural Conversation Questions for Client Intel Gathering
bestFor: ** First meetings, showings, and casual conversations
prompt:
You are a relationship-focused real estate agent. Write 15 conversation questions that naturally uncover personal details about clients -- details I can use to personalize future communication.

The questions should:
- Feel like genuine curiosity, not data collection
- Work in casual settings (driving to showings, waiting for an inspector, grabbing coffee)
- Cover: family life, career, hobbies, local favorites, upcoming life events
- Each include a 1-sentence note on WHEN to naturally ask it

Format: Numbered list. Each entry = the question + (in italics) the natural moment to ask it + [in brackets] what it unlocks for personalization.

Do NOT include anything that sounds like a survey ("On a scale of 1-5, how important is...").
Do NOT ask about finances in this list -- that's a separate, professional conversation.
Do NOT include more than 2 real-estate-specific questions. This is about knowing them as people.

Example:
1. "What does a perfect Saturday look like for you guys?" *(Ask while driving between showings)* [Unlocks: hobbies, family dynamics, neighborhood lifestyle preferences]
quickStart: Write 15 casual, genuine questions I can ask clients during showings and meetings to learn personal details for future personalization. No survey vibes. Include when to ask each one.

title: Personalized Milestone Message Templates
bestFor: ** Acknowledging client life events throughout the year
prompt:
You are a thoughtful real estate agent who stays connected with clients through personal milestones. Write 8 message templates for different life events.

For each, write an email version (under 100 words) and a text version (under 160 characters). Each must have [BRACKETS] showing where to insert personalized details.

The 8 milestones:
1. New baby
2. Job promotion or career change
3. Kid's graduation or school milestone
4. Home purchase anniversary (1 year)
5. Birthday
6. Wedding or engagement
7. Retirement
8. Pet adoption (yes, this matters)

Do NOT make every message circle back to real estate. At least 5 of the 8 should be purely personal with ZERO real estate mention.
Do NOT use generic phrases like "Wishing you all the best" or "Congratulations on this exciting milestone."
Do NOT start any message with "I hope this message finds you well."

Tone:
DO: "A BABY! [Name], this is the best news. [Partner's name] must be over the moon. When you're ready for visitors and need someone to bring takeout, text me."
DON'T: "Congratulations on the new addition to your family! As your home needs grow, remember I'm always here to help with your real estate needs."
quickStart: Write 8 short milestone messages (email + text versions) for: new baby, promotion, kid's graduation, home anniversary, birthday, wedding, retirement, pet adoption. Personal, warm, mostly NOT about real estate.

title: "Saw This and Thought of You" Message Generator
bestFor: ** Ongoing relationship maintenance between transactions
prompt:
You are a real estate agent who maintains relationships through genuine, personalized touchpoints. I want to send "saw this and thought of you" messages to past clients.

Here are 5 clients and something I know about each:
1. [CLIENT NAME]: [PERSONAL DETAIL -- e.g., "obsessed with Italian cooking"]
2. [CLIENT NAME]: [PERSONAL DETAIL -- e.g., "training for a marathon"]
3. [CLIENT NAME]: [PERSONAL DETAIL -- e.g., "just started a vegetable garden"]
4. [CLIENT NAME]: [PERSONAL DETAIL -- e.g., "huge Dodgers fan"]
5. [CLIENT NAME]: [PERSONAL DETAIL -- e.g., "loves live jazz"]

For each client, suggest ONE specific thing I could share (article, event, restaurant, tip) that connects to their interest AND my local market [CITY/AREA]. Then write the short text message (under 160 characters) I'd send with it.

Do NOT tie any of these to real estate or home buying/selling.
Do NOT use the phrase "thought of you" in more than 2 of the 5 messages -- vary the approach.
Do NOT make anything up. Flag where I need to look up a real local resource.

Tone:
DO: "Hey Marco -- there's an Italian street food pop-up at [LOCATION] this Saturday. Looked legit. You in?"
DON'T: "Hi Marco, I came across this article about Italian cuisine and thought of you. Hope you're enjoying your home!"
quickStart: I have 5 past clients with these interests: [LIST]. Suggest one specific local thing to share with each and write the text message. No real estate mentions. Under 160 characters each.
