title: Communication Preference Discovery Script
bestFor: ** New client onboarding
prompt:
You are a real estate agent who customizes communication for each client. Write a short, natural script I can use during our first meeting to discover how each client prefers to be contacted.

The script should uncover:
1. Preferred channel (text, email, phone, video call)
2. Preferred frequency (daily updates, weekly summaries, only when something important happens)
3. Detail level (quick bullet points or detailed analysis)
4. Best times to reach them
5. Who else should be included in communications (partner, parent, advisor)

Format: Write it as a casual 2-minute conversation -- my lines in bold, with transition notes. It should feel like getting to know them, not filling out a form.

Keep the whole script under 200 words.

Do NOT make it sound like a customer service intake form.
Do NOT list all options and ask them to choose -- guide them with simple either/or questions.
Do NOT skip the "who else" question -- it prevents problems later.

Tone:
DO: "Quick thing that'll save us both headaches -- are you a 'text me the highlights' person or a 'send me everything' person?"
DON'T: "I'd like to establish your communication preferences. Would you prefer email, text, phone, or video?"
quickStart: Write a casual 2-minute script for asking new clients how they prefer to be contacted -- channel, frequency, detail level, timing, and who to include. Conversational, not a form.

title: Personalized Market Update Email (Past Client)
bestFor: ** Quarterly or semi-annual past client touchpoints
prompt:
You are a real estate agent sending a market update to a past client -- but you want it to feel personal, not mass-mailed.

Client details:
- Name: [NAME]
- Purchased: [ADDRESS] in [DATE] for [PRICE]
- Personal detail: [SOMETHING YOU KNOW -- e.g., "they renovated the kitchen last spring"]
- Family situation: [e.g., "two kids, youngest starting kindergarten"]

Current market data for their area:
- [3-5 RELEVANT DATA POINTS -- median price, appreciation, days on market, inventory, etc.]

Write an email (under 150 words) that:
1. Opens with a personal reference -- NOT about real estate (1 sentence)
2. Shares 2-3 market insights specific to their neighborhood (not citywide stats)
3. Connects one data point to their personal situation (e.g., equity gain since purchase, impact of their renovation on value)
4. Ends casually -- no hard CTA, just an open door

Do NOT open with "I hope you're well" or any variation.
Do NOT use "your largest investment" or "building wealth through homeownership."
Do NOT include a referral ask in a market update -- keep channels clean.

Subject line: Reference their neighborhood by name, not "Market Update Q1."
quickStart: Write a 150-word personalized market update email for [PAST CLIENT NAME] who bought [ADDRESS] in [YEAR]. Include their neighborhood stats and one personal reference. No referral ask.

title: Same-Message, Different-Styles Adapter
bestFor: ** Agents with diverse client bases
prompt:
You are a communication specialist. Take the following message and rewrite it in 4 different styles to match different client communication preferences.

Original message: [PASTE YOUR MESSAGE -- e.g., a transaction update, market insight, or next-steps overview]

Rewrite in these 4 styles:

1. **The Quick-Hit Texter** -- Compress to under 160 characters. Only the essential point. Casual tone.
2. **The Data Nerd** -- Add relevant numbers, comparisons, and context. Email format, under 200 words. Professional but warm.
3. **The Relationship-First Client** -- Lead with personal connection, weave the information into a warm, conversational email. Under 150 words.
4. **The Anxious Buyer/Seller** -- Rewrite to lead with reassurance, explain what this means for them, and address the worry they're probably feeling. Under 200 words.

For each version, specify: the best delivery channel and the best time to send.

Do NOT change the core information -- same facts, different packaging.
Do NOT make the "anxious" version patronizing. Reassurance, not hand-holding.
Do NOT make any version longer than specified.
quickStart: Rewrite this message in 4 styles: quick text (160 chars), data-focused email (200 words), relationship-warm email (150 words), and reassuring email for anxious clients (200 words). Same info, different packaging.

title: Post-Transaction Voice Note Scripts
bestFor: ** Adding a personal touch to digital follow-up
prompt:
You are a real estate agent who uses voice notes (iMessage, WhatsApp) to stand out. Write 6 voice note scripts for post-transaction touchpoints.

Each script must be under 75 words (approximately 30 seconds when spoken).

The 6 scenarios:
1. One week after closing -- checking in on the move
2. One month after closing -- settling in
3. Three months after closing -- any home questions
4. Home anniversary (1 year)
5. After seeing a neighbor's home hit the market
6. Just because -- a genuine "thinking of you" with no agenda

For each, include:
- The script (exactly as I'd say it, including "Hey [Name]..." opener)
- One stage direction about tone (e.g., "smile while recording -- it comes through")

Do NOT write anything you wouldn't say out loud to a friend.
Do NOT include CTAs like "call me back" in more than 2 of the 6. Some should expect no response.
Do NOT mention referrals in any of these. Pure relationship building.

Tone:
DO: "Hey Sarah -- drove past your place on Elm yesterday and the garden looks AMAZING. You guys have totally made it yours. Love seeing it."
DON'T: "Hi Sarah, I wanted to reach out and check in on your property at 123 Elm Street. Please don't hesitate to contact me if you need anything."
quickStart: Write 6 voice note scripts (under 75 words each, 30 seconds spoken) for post-closing touchpoints: 1 week, 1 month, 3 months, 1 year, neighbor listed, and just-because. Warm, natural, mostly no CTA.

title: Personalized Home Maintenance Seasonal Reminder
bestFor: ** Quarterly past client touchpoints that provide genuine value
prompt:
You are a real estate agent sending a seasonal home maintenance reminder to a past client. Make it genuinely useful and personal.

Client details:
- Name: [NAME]
- Home type: [e.g., "1960s ranch with a large yard" or "2020 condo, 3rd floor"]
- Location/climate: [YOUR MARKET]
- Season: [CURRENT SEASON]
- Personal detail: [e.g., "they have a pool" or "first-time homeowner" or "they hate yard work"]

Write a short email (under 150 words) with:
1. A personal, warm opener (1 sentence referencing them, not the season)
2. A 5-item checklist of seasonal maintenance tasks SPECIFIC to their home type and climate
3. One "pro tip" most homeowners forget
4. A casual sign-off -- offer to recommend a contractor if they need one

Format the checklist as a clean numbered list.

Do NOT write a generic "spring cleaning" list. Tailor it to their home.
Do NOT include real estate market commentary. This is pure value.
Do NOT make it longer than 150 words. Brevity = it actually gets read.

Subject line: Specific and useful, like "5 things your ranch needs before winter" -- not "Seasonal Greetings from [Agent Name]."
quickStart: Write a 150-word seasonal maintenance email for [CLIENT NAME] who owns a [HOME TYPE] in [MARKET]. 5-item checklist specific to their home, one pro tip, offer to recommend contractors. No market talk.

title: Post-Price-Reduction Seller Check-In
bestFor: ** Following up with your seller after a price reduction, balancing empathy with forward momentum
prompt:
You are a listing agent who just guided a seller through a price reduction. The conversation was hard, and now you need to follow up in a way that keeps them confident in you and motivated about the sale.

Context:
- Seller: [NAME/DESCRIPTION]
- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Original list price: [PRICE]
- New price: [PRICE]
- Reason for reduction: [e.g., "no offers in 21 days," "showing feedback consistently cited price," "market shifted since listing"]
- Seller's emotional state: [e.g., "frustrated but understood," "reluctant and worried," "angry at the market"]
- Showing feedback themes: [SUMMARIZE 3-5 COMMON BUYER/AGENT COMMENTS]

Write 3 messages:

1. **Same-day text** (within 2 hours of the price change going live, under 200 characters): Reassure them, signal that you're already in motion, and set the tone that this is a strategic move -- not a defeat.

2. **48-hour email** (under 200 words): Share early results of the price change -- new saves, showing requests, agent inquiries. Include:
   - A brief market context section (2-3 sentences positioning WHY this price point works, using real data)
   - A summarized showing feedback section (what buyers have consistently said, framed constructively -- not as criticism of their home)
   - A next-steps section with 1-2 specific actions you're taking this week

3. **7-day check-in text** (under 200 characters): Share one concrete result -- a showing booked, renewed agent interest, a comparable sale that validates the new price. Keep it forward-looking.

Do NOT use phrases like "the market has spoken" or "we had to be realistic." Frame the reduction as strategic, not as a loss.
Do NOT pile on more bad news. Even if activity is slow, find a genuine positive to lead with.
Do NOT suggest another reduction in any of these messages. One adjustment at a time.

Tone:
DO: "Price went live 30 minutes ago -- already seeing new saves on Zillow. The phone should start ringing. I'll keep you posted as showings come in."
DON'T: "As discussed, we've adjusted the price to better align with current market conditions. I hope this will generate more interest."
quickStart: Write a same-day text (200 chars), 48-hour email (200 words), and 7-day text (200 chars) for my seller [NAME] after reducing price on [ADDRESS] from [OLD PRICE] to [NEW PRICE]. Empathetic, forward-looking, include market context and showing feedback summary. No defeat language.

title: Past Client Segmentation by Referral Potential
bestFor: ** Agents with 50+ past clients who want to prioritize outreach
prompt:
You are a referral marketing strategist. Help me segment my past client database into referral tiers so I can focus my energy wisely.

Create a scoring framework with 4 tiers:

1. **Super Connectors** -- Criteria: large social networks, community involvement, already referred once, vocal advocates
2. **Natural Referrers** -- Criteria: expressed willingness, highly satisfied, socially active but less connected
3. **Passive Satisfied** -- Criteria: happy with their experience but haven't referred, need activation
4. **Needs Attention** -- Criteria: may have had service issues, lost contact, or cooled on the relationship

For each tier, provide:
- 4 identifying characteristics (how I spot who belongs here)
- The recommended annual touchpoint count
- The primary strategy (1 sentence)
- One example outreach message (under 100 words)

Present as a table with columns: Tier | Characteristics | Touchpoints/Year | Strategy | Example Message.

Do NOT suggest stalking social media to score people. Use information from natural interactions.
Do NOT create more than 4 tiers -- complexity kills follow-through.
Do NOT make "Needs Attention" feel like a lost cause. Include a re-engagement angle.
quickStart: Create a 4-tier past client segmentation framework for referral potential: Super Connectors, Natural Referrers, Passive Satisfied, and Needs Attention. Table format with criteria, touchpoint frequency, strategy, and sample message for each.

title: 12-Month Past Client Touchpoint Calendar
bestFor: ** Building a structured annual relationship nurture plan
prompt:
You are a real estate relationship strategist. Design a 12-month touchpoint calendar for past client nurture. My market is [YOUR MARKET/CITY].

For each month, provide:
1. **Primary touchpoint:** What it is, delivery channel, and a 2-sentence content summary
2. **Theme:** The value it provides to the homeowner (not to me)
3. **Referral seed:** A subtle, natural way referrals are woven in (not a direct ask) -- include this in only 4 of the 12 months

Format as a table: Month | Theme | Touchpoint | Channel | Referral Seed (Y/N)

Guidelines:
- Mix channels: 5 emails, 2 texts, 2 voice notes, 1 handwritten card, 1 small gift/pop-by, 1 phone call
- At least 4 touchpoints should be purely about THEM (no real estate angle)
- Content should be seasonally relevant to [YOUR MARKET]
- No two consecutive months should use the same channel

Do NOT front-load all the referral asks. Spread them across the year.
Do NOT include a touchpoint in December that's a generic holiday card. Be more creative.
Do NOT plan more than one touchpoint per month. Consistency beats volume.
quickStart: Create a 12-month past client touchpoint calendar for [MARKET]. One touchpoint per month, mixed channels, mostly value-driven, referral seeds in only 4 months. Table format.

title: Non-Cringe Referral Ask Scripts
bestFor: ** Agents who hate asking for referrals
prompt:
You are a referral strategy expert. Write 6 different ways to ask past clients for referrals that DON'T make either of us cringe.

For each approach, provide:
- **When to use it** (the specific context or trigger)
- **The script** -- text, email, and in-person versions (text under 160 chars, email under 100 words, in-person under 50 words spoken)
- **Why it works** (1 sentence on the psychology)

The 6 approaches:
1. The "helping your friends" frame -- position referring as a favor to their FRIEND, not to you
2. The "specific ask" -- name a type of person, not "anyone who..."
3. The "earned moment" -- after delivering exceptional value
4. The "reciprocity" -- after you've given them something valuable
5. The "third-party proof" -- share a success story, then open the door
6. The "permission-based" -- ask if they'd be open to it, don't assume

Do NOT use the word "referral" in any client-facing language. People don't "refer" friends -- they "introduce" or "connect" them.
Do NOT use guilt ("I built my business on referrals" or "Referrals are the highest compliment").
Do NOT ask for referrals in the same message as a home anniversary or milestone -- it cheapens the personal touch.

Tone:
DO: "If you ever have a friend stressing about finding a place, I'd love to help take that off their plate. Just shoot me a text with their name."
DON'T: "I would greatly appreciate it if you could refer any friends, family, or colleagues who may be considering buying or selling real estate."
quickStart: Write 6 non-cringe ways to ask for referrals in text (160 chars), email (100 words), and in-person (50 words) formats. Never use the word "referral." No guilt trips.

title: "I Just Got a Referral" Thank-You Sequence
bestFor: ** Every time someone sends you a referral
prompt:
You are a real estate agent who takes referral gratitude seriously. Someone just referred a potential client to me. Write my complete thank-you sequence.

Context:
- Referrer: [NAME, RELATIONSHIP -- e.g., "past client who bought with me 2 years ago"]
- Referred person: [NAME, SITUATION]
- How the referral came: [e.g., "texted me their friend's number," "told their coworker to call me"]

Write these 3 messages:

1. **Immediate thank-you** (within 2 hours): Text message, under 160 characters. Genuine, excited, specific.
2. **Progress update** (after first meeting with the referred person): Email, under 100 words. Let the referrer know their friend is in good hands WITHOUT violating the new client's privacy.
3. **Outcome thank-you** (when the referral closes OR if it doesn't work out): Handwritten card content, under 75 words. Grateful regardless of outcome.

Do NOT promise to "take great care of them" -- show it through updates instead.
Do NOT share private details about the referred person's transaction with the referrer.
Do NOT skip step 2. The progress update is what turns a one-time referrer into a repeat referrer.

Tone:
DO: "YOU. Thank you. Just got off the phone with [Name] and they're great. I owe you one."
DON'T: "Thank you for the referral of [Name]. I will provide them with the highest level of service."
quickStart: Write 3 thank-you messages for a referral: immediate text (160 chars), progress update email (100 words), and closing handwritten card (75 words). Genuine and warm, no privacy violations.

title: Pop-By Gift Ideas with Conversation Starters
bestFor: ** Quarterly in-person touchpoints with top clients
prompt:
You are a creative real estate agent. Design 4 seasonal pop-by gift concepts (one per quarter) for past clients.

For each pop-by, provide:
1. **The gift** (under $15, memorable, useful -- not another branded pen)
2. **The tag/note** (a clever 1-liner connecting the gift to the season, under 15 words)
3. **The conversation starter** (what to say at the door that naturally opens a 2-3 minute chat, under 30 words)
4. **Season:** Spring, Summer, Fall, Winter

Requirements:
- At least 2 gifts should be consumable (food, drink, etc.)
- At least 1 should be family/kid-friendly
- None should have my branding on the actual gift (the tag can have my name)
- The door conversation should NOT mention real estate in 3 of the 4

Do NOT suggest wine (liability issues in some areas, and not everyone drinks).
Do NOT suggest anything that requires explanation. Simple = memorable.
Do NOT suggest gift cards -- they're convenient but forgettable.

Format: Table with columns: Season | Gift | Tag Line | Door Conversation Starter
quickStart: Design 4 seasonal pop-by gifts (under $15 each) with clever tag lines and natural door conversation starters. No branded items, no wine, no gift cards. Table format.

title: Annual Home Value Update That Clients Actually Want
bestFor: ** Annual touchpoint with every past buyer client
prompt:
You are a real estate agent preparing a personalized annual home value update for a past client.

Client details:
- Name: [NAME]
- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Purchase date: [DATE]
- Purchase price: [PRICE]
- Current estimated value: [YOUR ESTIMATE OR ZESTIMATE RANGE]
- Notable changes: [e.g., "they added a deck," "new school built nearby," "similar home on their street sold for $X"]

Write a short email (under 200 words) that:
1. Opens with genuine enthusiasm about their equity growth (or honest context if the market is flat/down)
2. Shows 2-3 key data points in a simple table: purchase price, current estimate, equity gained, percent appreciation
3. Mentions 1-2 factors driving value in their specific neighborhood
4. Closes warmly -- NOT with a "thinking of selling?" pitch

Do NOT say "your home is your biggest investment."
Do NOT include a CRM-generated home value report -- this is personal commentary.
Do NOT pivot to a listing pitch. This builds trust, not transactions. The transaction will come when they're ready.

Subject line: Something they'd open out of curiosity, like "[Address] -- year 3 check-in" not "Your Annual Home Valuation Report."
quickStart: Write a 200-word personal home value update email for [CLIENT] who bought [ADDRESS] in [YEAR] for [PRICE], now worth approximately [ESTIMATE]. Include a simple equity table. No listing pitch.

title: Client Appreciation Event Invitation
bestFor: ** Annual events that generate referrals organically
prompt:
You are a real estate agent planning a client appreciation event. Write the invitation copy and follow-up messages.

Event details:
- Type: [e.g., "backyard BBQ," "movie night at a local park," "holiday cookie decorating for families"]
- Date/Location: [DETAILS]
- Who's invited: Past clients and their friends/family

Write:
1. **Email invitation** (under 150 words): Warm, exciting, emphasizes fun -- not networking. Include a "bring a friend" element that feels natural, not forced.
2. **Text reminder** (3 days before, under 160 characters): Casual and excited.
3. **Post-event thank-you text** (next day, under 160 characters): Reference one specific moment from the event.

Do NOT call it a "client appreciation event" in client-facing copy. Call it what it is: a BBQ, a movie night, a party.
Do NOT make the invitation sound like a corporate event. This is a gathering, not a gala.
Do NOT use the word "networking" anywhere. People come for fun, not to be sold to.

Tone:
DO: "We're throwing a backyard movie night and you're invited. Bring the kids, blankets, and someone who'd enjoy free popcorn and a good time."
DON'T: "You are cordially invited to our Annual Client Appreciation Event. We value your business and look forward to celebrating our partnership."
quickStart: Write invitation email (150 words), reminder text (160 chars), and post-event thank-you text (160 chars) for my [EVENT TYPE] for past clients. Fun tone, "bring a friend" element, zero corporate language.

title: "Stay Top of Mind" Content Ideas Generator
bestFor: ** When you're stuck on what to send past clients
variables: ANY LOCAL NEWS, EVENT, OR TREND
prompt:
You are a creative content strategist for real estate agents. I'm stuck on what to send my past clients this month. Help me brainstorm.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
Current season: [SEASON]
Something happening locally: [ANY LOCAL NEWS, EVENT, OR TREND]
My past clients are mostly: [DEMOGRAPHIC -- e.g., "young families," "retirees," "mixed"]

Generate 10 content ideas I could send as a short email, text, or social media share. For each idea:
1. The content concept (1 sentence)
2. The format (text, email, or social post)
3. The subject line or opening hook
4. Why it works (1 sentence)

Rules:
- At least 4 ideas should have ZERO connection to real estate
- At least 2 should be hyperlocal (about our specific area)
- At least 1 should be interactive (asks them to reply or engage)
- No more than 2 should be market/home-related

Do NOT suggest "monthly market updates" or "home maintenance tips" -- those are covered in other prompts.
Do NOT suggest content that requires professional production. I should be able to send it today.
Do NOT repeat the same format more than 3 times.
quickStart: Give me 10 quick content ideas to send past clients this month in [MARKET/SEASON]. At least 4 non-real-estate ideas, 2 hyperlocal, 1 interactive. Mix of text, email, social formats.

title: Client Communication Frequency Matrix
bestFor: ** Setting up a system-wide contact frequency plan
prompt:
You are a sales cadence expert. Create a communication frequency matrix for a real estate agent managing multiple relationship types.

Build a table with these rows (client stages):
1. Hot lead (actively searching, responded recently)
2. Warm lead (interested but not urgent)
3. Cool lead (expressed interest months ago, gone quiet)
4. Active buyer under contract
5. Active seller with listing
6. Post-close: 0-90 days
7. Post-close: 3-12 months
8. Past client: 1-3 years
9. Past client: 3+ years

Columns:
- Contacts per month
- Primary channel
- Secondary channel
- Content type (what to talk about)
- Escalation trigger (what signals I should increase frequency)

Do NOT recommend more than 8 contacts/month for any category. More isn't better -- it's annoying.
Do NOT recommend the same channel for every stage. Mix it up.
Do NOT suggest "daily" contact for any stage. Even hot leads need breathing room.

Present as a clean, readable table.
quickStart: Create a contact frequency table for 9 client stages (hot lead through 3+ year past client). Show contacts/month, channels, content type, and escalation triggers for each.

title: Engagement Signal Decoder
bestFor: ** Knowing when to increase or decrease follow-up intensity
prompt:
You are a lead engagement analyst. Create a guide that helps me read client engagement signals and adjust my follow-up frequency accordingly.

Create two lists:

**INCREASE frequency when you see these signals** (list 8 signals):
For each: the signal, what it likely means, and the specific action to take.

**DECREASE frequency when you see these signals** (list 8 signals):
For each: the signal, what it likely means, and the specific action to take.

Include signals from: email behavior, text responses, website activity, social media, and conversation tone.

Format: Two separate tables. Columns: Signal | What It Means | Action

Do NOT suggest increasing frequency past a maximum of 3 contacts per week for any reason.
Do NOT frame decreasing frequency as "giving up" -- frame it as strategic patience.
Do NOT include signals that require expensive tracking tools. Stick to what I can observe naturally or with basic CRM data.
quickStart: List 8 engagement signals that mean "increase follow-up" and 8 that mean "decrease follow-up." For each, explain what it means and what action to take. Two tables.

title: Personality-Based Follow-Up: The Driver
bestFor: ** Fast-moving, no-nonsense clients
prompt:
You are a communication strategist. Write 5 follow-up message templates optimized for "Driver" personality types -- clients who are decisive, time-conscious, and want facts without fluff.

The 5 scenarios:
1. After a showing (text)
2. Market update (email)
3. Requesting a decision (text)
4. Sharing a new listing (email)
5. Post-close check-in (text)

For each, write the message AND explain why this style works for Drivers in 1 sentence.

Rules for all messages:
- Texts: under 100 characters. Every word must earn its place.
- Emails: under 75 words. Lead with the point, then support it.
- No pleasantries or warm-up sentences. Get to it.
- Use numbers and data wherever possible.
- One CTA maximum. Make it binary (yes/no, this/that).

Do NOT use emoji or exclamation marks with Drivers.
Do NOT ask open-ended questions. Give them options to choose from.
Do NOT pad messages with "hope you're well" or "when you get a chance."

Tone:
DO: "3-bed on Oak. $425K. 4 days on market. Comps support price. Want to see it tomorrow at 2?"
DON'T: "Hi! I hope your week is going great! I found a lovely property I think you might be interested in..."
quickStart: Write 5 ultra-short follow-up messages for no-nonsense, data-driven clients. Texts under 100 chars, emails under 75 words. No fluff, no pleasantries. Five scenarios: post-showing, market update, decision request, new listing, post-close.

title: Personality-Based Follow-Up: The Analytical
bestFor: ** Research-heavy, detail-oriented clients
prompt:
You are a communication strategist. Write 5 follow-up message templates optimized for "Analytical" personality types -- clients who want data, need processing time, and make decisions methodically.

The 5 scenarios:
1. After a showing (email)
2. Market update (email)
3. When they're taking a long time to decide (text)
4. Sharing a new listing (email)
5. Post-close check-in (email)

For each, write the message AND explain why this style works for Analyticals in 1 sentence.

Rules for all messages:
- Emails: under 200 words but data-rich. Use bullet points, numbers, and comparisons.
- Texts: under 160 characters. Acknowledge their process, don't rush it.
- Include at least one data point or comparison in every email.
- Offer additional information proactively ("I also pulled the tax history if you want it").
- Never rush them. Give explicit timelines instead ("The listing has been up 6 days; average DOM in this area is 14").

Do NOT say "trust your gut" to an Analytical. They don't work that way.
Do NOT send information without context. Raw data without interpretation is noise.
Do NOT follow up more than once between their responses. Give them space to process.

Tone:
DO: "I ran the numbers on both properties. Here's how they compare on price/sqft, tax burden, and projected appreciation. Take your time -- this data isn't going anywhere."
DON'T: "Which one felt right to you? Sometimes you just know!"
quickStart: Write 5 data-rich follow-up messages for analytical, methodical clients. Emails under 200 words with numbers and comparisons. Texts under 160 chars. Five scenarios: post-showing, market update, slow decision, new listing, post-close.

title: Personality-Based Follow-Up: The Relationship Builder
bestFor: ** Warm, people-oriented clients who decide based on trust
prompt:
You are a communication strategist. Write 5 follow-up message templates optimized for "Relationship Builder" personality types -- clients who value personal connection, warmth, and trust above data.

The 5 scenarios:
1. After a showing (text)
2. Market update (email)
3. Checking in during a slow search (text)
4. Sharing a new listing (text + email combo)
5. Post-close check-in (voice note script, under 75 words)

For each, write the message AND explain why this style works for Relationship Builders in 1 sentence.

Rules for all messages:
- Lead with personal connection before business content
- Reference shared experiences or previous conversations
- Use their name and family members' names naturally
- Emails under 150 words, texts under 200 characters, voice note under 75 words
- Warmth is not fluff -- every personal touch should be genuine

Do NOT be purely transactional. Even a listing share should include a personal line.
Do NOT send automated-feeling messages to this type. They'll feel it immediately.
Do NOT overwhelm with data. Give one or two key points and offer to discuss the rest in person.

Tone:
DO: "Hey Melissa -- how did Jake's soccer tournament go? Also, a house came up on Birch that has that wraparound porch you've been dreaming about. Want to swing by Saturday after the game?"
DON'T: "New listing alert: 456 Birch St. 3BR/2BA, $389K. Let me know if you'd like to schedule a showing."
quickStart: Write 5 warm, personal follow-up messages for relationship-oriented clients. Lead with personal connection, then business. Texts under 200 chars, emails under 150 words, one voice note under 75 words.

title: Personality-Based Follow-Up: The Anxious Overthinker
bestFor: ** First-time buyers, risk-averse clients, major life transitions
prompt:
You are a communication strategist. Write 5 follow-up message templates optimized for "Anxious Overthinker" types -- clients who worry about making mistakes, need reassurance, and may stall or second-guess decisions.

The 5 scenarios:
1. After a showing when they liked something but seem scared (text)
2. After submitting an offer -- the waiting period (text)
3. When they're paralyzed by too many options (email)
4. After home inspection reveals issues (email)
5. Post-close reassurance (text)

For each, write the message AND explain why this approach works for anxious clients in 1 sentence.

Rules for all messages:
- Lead with reassurance, then information
- Normalize their feelings ("Every buyer feels this at this stage")
- Provide clear next steps -- ambiguity fuels anxiety
- Texts under 200 characters, emails under 150 words
- End with certainty, not open-ended questions

Do NOT dismiss their concerns ("Don't worry about it!").
Do NOT pile on information -- too much data increases anxiety.
Do NOT use urgency language. Ever. With this type, urgency creates paralysis, not action.

Tone:
DO: "That inspection report looks scarier than it is. The roof note? Totally normal for a 2005 build. Here's what we do next -- and it's straightforward."
DON'T: "The inspection found several issues we need to address. Please review the full report and let me know how you'd like to proceed."
quickStart: Write 5 reassuring follow-up messages for anxious, overthinking clients. Lead with comfort, normalize feelings, give clear next steps. Texts under 200 chars, emails under 150 words. Zero urgency language.

title: Personality Type Quick Assessment Guide
bestFor: ** First interactions with new clients
prompt:
You are a behavioral communication expert. Create a quick-reference guide that helps me identify a client's communication personality type within the first conversation.

The 4 types: Driver, Analytical, Relationship Builder, Anxious Overthinker.

For each type, provide:
1. **3 things they say** (actual phrases or question styles that reveal their type)
2. **3 behaviors you'll notice** (how they act during showings, how fast they respond, how they make decisions)
3. **The biggest mistake agents make with this type** (1 sentence)
4. **The golden rule for communicating with them** (1 sentence)

Format as a 4-column reference card / table I can save on my phone:
Type | What They Say | How They Act | Golden Rule

Keep the total output under 400 words. This is a quick-glance reference, not a psychology textbook.

Do NOT present these as rigid boxes -- note that people are blends and may shift types depending on the situation.
Do NOT use clinical or academic language. Keep it practical.
Do NOT include more than 3 examples per category. Constraint = usability.
quickStart: Create a quick-reference table for identifying 4 client personality types (Driver, Analytical, Relationship Builder, Anxious Overthinker) based on what they say, how they act, and the golden rule for each.

title: Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week Send Guide
bestFor: ** Optimizing when you hit send on any message
prompt:
You are a communication timing strategist. Create a send-time guide for real estate follow-up across different channels.

Build a table with:
- Rows: Email, Text, Phone Call, Voice Note, Social Media DM
- Columns: Best Times (specific hours), Worst Times, Best Days, Worst Days, Why

Also include a section on:
1. **3 timing rules of thumb** (general principles that apply across all channels)
2. **2 exceptions** (when to break the rules -- e.g., hot market situations, time-sensitive info)

Keep the entire output under 300 words total.

Do NOT recommend sending anything between 9 PM and 7 AM unless it's a scheduled send.
Do NOT suggest the same optimal time for every channel -- they're genuinely different.
Do NOT over-complicate it. Simple rules I can remember are better than a complex algorithm I'll ignore.

Note: Include a caveat that individual client preferences always override general guidelines.
quickStart: Create a send-time table for 5 channels (email, text, phone, voice note, DM) showing best times, worst times, best days, and worst days. Plus 3 general timing rules.

title: New Buyer Lead 10-Email Drip Campaign
bestFor: ** Automated nurture for new buyer leads over 30 days
prompt:
You are an email copywriter who specializes in real estate drip campaigns that feel personal. Write a complete 10-email drip campaign for new buyer leads over 30 days.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My specialty/niche: [e.g., "first-time buyers," "luxury condos," "suburban families"]

For each email, provide:
1. **Send day** (day 0 through day 30)
2. **Subject line** (2 A/B versions)
3. **Preview text** (under 50 characters)
4. **Email body** (under 150 words)
5. **CTA** (one per email, clear and specific)
6. **Purpose in the sequence** (1 sentence: what this email accomplishes)

Campaign arc:
- Emails 1-2: Introduce myself, set expectations, deliver immediate value
- Emails 3-4: Educate on the buying process in my market
- Emails 5-6: Build trust through proof and local expertise
- Emails 7-8: Address common objections and fears
- Emails 9-10: Clear, direct invitation to take the next step + graceful exit if they're not ready

Do NOT use "Just checking in" as a subject line or opening.
Do NOT write emails that sound mass-mailed. Each should read like it was written for one person.
Do NOT make every email about real estate. At least 2 should lead with lifestyle, local content, or personal value.

Tone across the campaign:
DO: "I know you're probably getting emails from 11 agents right now. I won't add to the noise -- just sharing one thing I think you'll actually find useful."
DON'T: "As your dedicated real estate professional, I wanted to provide you with valuable market insights."

Each email MUST be under 150 words. If I wouldn't read it in 30 seconds, my leads won't either.
quickStart: Write a 10-email, 30-day buyer lead drip campaign for [MARKET]. Each email under 150 words, 2 A/B subject lines. Arc: introduce, educate, build trust, handle objections, invite action. Personal tone, not mass-mailed.

title: New Seller Lead 10-Email Drip Campaign
bestFor: ** Automated nurture for potential sellers over 30 days
prompt:
You are an email copywriter for real estate. Write a 10-email drip campaign for new seller leads over 30 days.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
My average list-to-sale ratio: [PERCENTAGE]
My average days on market: [NUMBER]

For each email, provide:
1. **Send day** (day 0 through day 30)
2. **Subject line** (2 A/B versions)
3. **Email body** (under 150 words)
4. **CTA** (one per email)
5. **Purpose** (1 sentence)

Campaign arc:
- Emails 1-2: Acknowledge their timeline, share one market insight that matters to sellers
- Emails 3-4: Demonstrate my marketing approach and local expertise
- Emails 5-6: Social proof -- past seller success stories
- Emails 7-8: Address seller fears (pricing wrong, showing disruption, not getting offers)
- Emails 9-10: No-pressure consultation invitation + value if they decide to wait

Do NOT pressure sellers who aren't ready. The campaign should nurture, not push.
Do NOT bash other agents or FSBOs. Differentiate through value, not negativity.
Do NOT use "I'd love to help you maximize your home's value" -- everyone says that.

Each email under 150 words.

Tone:
DO: "Your neighbor on Oak sold in 8 days last month. Not saying that's your timeline -- but if you're curious what's happening on your street, I have the data."
DON'T: "The market is hot and now is the perfect time to sell! Contact me for a free home valuation."
quickStart: Write a 10-email, 30-day seller lead drip campaign for [MARKET]. Under 150 words each. Arc: market insight, expertise demo, social proof, fear handling, low-pressure CTA. Include A/B subject lines.

title: Past Client Annual Nurture Drip (12 Emails)
bestFor: ** Automated annual touchpoint system for past clients
prompt:
You are an email copywriter. Write 12 emails (one per month) for an annual past client nurture campaign.

My market: [CITY/AREA]
Typical past client profile: [DESCRIPTION -- e.g., "homeowners, 30s-40s, families"]

For each email:
1. **Month and theme**
2. **Subject line**
3. **Email body** (under 150 words)
4. **The value** (what makes this worth reading -- 1 sentence)

Campaign rules:
- Each email should feel like the next chapter of an ongoing relationship, not a standalone blast
- No more than 3 of 12 should mention real estate directly
- At least 4 should provide practical homeowner value (tips, local resources, seasonal advice)
- Include a natural, soft referral seed in ONLY 2 of the 12 emails
- Mix tones: some informative, some warm, some fun
- The 12th email should be a genuine year-end reflection that deepens the relationship

Do NOT send the same email everyone sends in January (New Year goals) or December (holiday card).
Do NOT include a referral ask in more than 2 emails. Restraint builds trust.
Do NOT use identical formatting for every email. Vary structure: some short, some with lists, some conversational.

Each email under 150 words. If it feels like a newsletter, rewrite it as a personal note.
quickStart: Write 12 monthly emails for a past client nurture campaign in [MARKET]. Under 150 words each. Mostly non-real-estate content. Referral seeds in only 2. Personal note tone, not newsletter.

title: Cold Lead Re-Engagement Email Sequence (5 Emails)
bestFor: ** Leads who stopped responding 2+ months ago
prompt:
You are an email strategist specializing in re-engaging dormant leads. Write a 5-email re-engagement sequence for leads who went silent.

Typical silent lead profile: [DESCRIBE -- e.g., "buyer leads who inquired 3-6 months ago and stopped responding"]
My market: [CITY/AREA]

For each email:
1. **Send day** (spread over 30 days)
2. **Subject line** (designed to break the pattern -- not "checking in")
3. **Email body** (under 120 words)
4. **Strategy** (the psychological principle in 1 sentence)

The 5-email arc:
1. **The Market Shift** -- Something changed that gives them a reason to re-engage
2. **The Quick Question** -- One simple, easy-to-answer question that re-opens dialogue
3. **The Proof** -- A short success story of someone in a similar situation
4. **The Honest Note** -- Direct, transparent: "Not sure if buying is still on your radar..."
5. **The Clean Break** -- "Should I stop sending updates?" (counterintuitive but effective)

Do NOT guilt-trip them for going silent.
Do NOT reference how many times you've followed up.
Do NOT assume they still want to buy/sell. Leave the door open for "my situation changed."

Tone:
DO: "Hey -- not sure if you're still exploring the move. Either way, thought you'd find this interesting: [insight]."
DON'T: "I've tried reaching you several times and haven't heard back. I want to make sure you don't miss out on current market opportunities."

Each email under 120 words. Brevity signals confidence.
quickStart: Write a 5-email re-engagement sequence for silent leads, sent over 30 days. Under 120 words each. Arc: market shift, quick question, success story, honest check-in, clean break. No guilt trips.

title: Break-Up Email That Reactivates Leads
bestFor: ** Final email to unresponsive leads before removing them from active follow-up
prompt:
You are an email strategist. Write the ultimate "break-up" email for leads who haven't responded to anything.

Context: This lead [DESCRIPTION] last engaged [TIMEFRAME] ago. I've sent [NUMBER] messages with no response.

Write:
1. **The email** (under 100 words): honest, respectful, zero guilt. Give them clear options for how to respond.
2. **Subject line** (5 A/B versions): each designed to trigger curiosity or a reaction.
3. **Response handling** -- write a short reply template for each of these 4 common responses:
   - "Actually, we're still interested!" (under 50 words)
   - "Yes, please remove me." (under 30 words)
   - "Our situation changed -- here's what's new." (under 50 words)
   - No response (what to do -- 2 sentences)

Do NOT be passive-aggressive ("Since I haven't heard from you...").
Do NOT make them feel bad for being silent. Life happens.
Do NOT actually remove them from your database after no response. Move them to a long-term nurture list.

Tone:
DO: "Quick honest note -- I haven't heard from you in a while and I don't want to be that agent who keeps emailing into the void. Three options: [A], [B], or [C]. Any of them works for me."
DON'T: "This is my final attempt to reach you. If I don't hear back, I'll assume you are no longer interested in purchasing a home."
quickStart: Write a 100-word break-up email for unresponsive leads with 5 A/B subject lines and reply templates for 4 common responses. Honest, no guilt, clear options.

title: Email Subject Line Generator for Follow-Up
bestFor: ** When your emails aren't getting opened
prompt:
You are an email subject line specialist. Generate 30 subject lines for real estate follow-up emails, organized by category.

Categories (5 subject lines each):
1. **Post-showing follow-up** -- reference specific property details
2. **Market updates** -- make data feel personal and urgent-to-know
3. **Past client check-ins** -- warm, personal, non-salesy
4. **Re-engagement** -- pattern-interrupt for leads who've gone silent
5. **Value-add content** -- make tips/resources feel unmissable
6. **Referral-related** -- subtle, not needy

Rules for all subject lines:
- Under 50 characters (critical for mobile)
- No ALL CAPS or excessive punctuation
- No spam trigger words (free, guaranteed, act now, limited time)
- At least 10 should include a personalization bracket [NAME] or [DETAIL]
- At least 5 should be questions
- At least 5 should create curiosity gaps

Format: Numbered list grouped by category.

Do NOT use "Just checking in," "Following up," "Hope you're well," or "Quick question" (unless the question is actually quick and specific).
Do NOT use clickbait that the email body can't deliver on.
Do NOT repeat structures -- each subject line should use a different approach.
quickStart: Generate 30 email subject lines for real estate follow-up in 6 categories (5 each). Under 50 characters, mobile-optimized, no spam words. Mix of questions, curiosity gaps, and personalization.

title: Lost Offer Recovery Sequence
bestFor: ** When your buyer loses in a multiple-offer situation and needs to stay motivated
prompt:
You are a buyer's agent. My client just lost a property they were emotionally invested in. They submitted an offer and didn't get it. I need to help them process the disappointment and keep them motivated to continue their search.

Context:
- Client: [NAME/DESCRIPTION -- e.g., "first-time buyer couple, been searching 3 months"]
- Property lost: [ADDRESS, KEY FEATURES THEY LOVED]
- Offer details: [WHAT THEY OFFERED vs. LIST PRICE]
- How they lost: [e.g., "outbid by $15K," "seller chose all-cash offer," "multiple offers -- we were #3," "unknown"]
- Emotional state: [e.g., "crushed -- this was 'the one,'" "frustrated -- third offer lost," "discouraged but trying to stay positive"]
- Market context: [e.g., "competitive market, 80% of homes getting multiple offers" or "this was unusual -- most homes aren't going over ask"]

Write a complete recovery sequence:

1. **Same-day text** (within 1 hour of the news, under 200 characters): Acknowledge the disappointment honestly. No silver linings yet -- just empathy and solidarity. Let them know you're in this with them.

2. **Next-day email** (under 200 words): Now it's time for perspective. Include:
   - Validation of their feelings (2 sentences -- this IS hard and their frustration is reasonable)
   - Honest context about why this happened (market dynamics, not their fault)
   - One thing they did RIGHT in this offer (their agent work, their decisiveness, their financial prep)
   - A forward-looking close that hints at what's coming without being pushy

3. **7-day recovery sequence plan** -- outline 5 touchpoints over the next 7 days:
   | Day | Channel | Message summary (1-2 sentences) | Purpose |
   For each touchpoint, write the full message content.
   - Day 2: A new listing or pocket listing that matches their criteria (text, under 160 chars)
   - Day 3: A brief "what we learned" note -- how this loss sharpens your strategy for the next offer (email, under 120 words)
   - Day 4: Something non-real-estate and personal (text, under 160 chars)
   - Day 5: A success story of a past client who lost an offer and then found something better (email, under 150 words)
   - Day 7: A "ready when you are" check-in with 1-2 new options to look at (text, under 200 chars)

Do NOT say "everything happens for a reason" or "the right house is out there." Platitudes insult their intelligence.
Do NOT blame them for the loss (should have offered more, should have waived inspection, etc.).
Do NOT disappear and wait for them to reach out. Silence after a loss feels like abandonment.

Tone:
DO: "That one stings. I'm not going to pretend it doesn't. When you're ready, I have some thoughts on what's coming on the market next week -- but tonight, just be bummed. You earned it."
DON'T: "Unfortunately, our offer was not accepted. However, I am confident that we will find an even better property for you."
quickStart: Write a lost-offer recovery sequence for my buyer [NAME] who just lost [ADDRESS] in a multiple-offer situation. Include: same-day text (200 chars), next-day email (200 words), and a 5-touchpoint 7-day recovery plan with full message content. Empathetic, honest, forward-looking.

title: "They Chose Another Agent" Graceful Exit
bestFor: ** When a lead or prospect tells you they've decided to work with a different agent
prompt:
You are a real estate agent who just learned that a lead or prospect has chosen to work with another agent. This is a high-emotion moment -- you're disappointed, maybe frustrated, but how you respond right now determines whether they come back to you when it doesn't work out (and whether they refer you despite choosing someone else).

Context:
- Who: [NAME/DESCRIPTION -- e.g., "buyer lead I'd been working with for 3 weeks," "seller who interviewed me and two other agents," "past client who listed with someone else this time"]
- How they told you: [e.g., "texted me," "told me on the phone," "I found out from seeing the listing online," "ghosted and I figured it out"]
- Your relationship level: [e.g., "we'd done 4 showings together," "just one phone consultation," "I sold them their current home 5 years ago"]
- Why they chose someone else (if known): [e.g., "family friend in the business," "lower commission," "they wanted a different approach," "unknown"]

Write 3 messages for different response scenarios:

1. **The gracious immediate response** (text or email, under 150 words): Respond to their news with class. Wish them well genuinely. Leave the door wide open without desperation. The goal is for them to read this and think "wow, I might have made a mistake."

2. **The 90-day check-in** (text, under 160 characters): A light, genuine touchpoint 90 days later. NOT "how's it going with your new agent?" -- something natural that keeps you on their radar without making it about the other agent.

3. **The long-game annual touchpoint** (email, under 120 words): A message for your annual nurture rotation. Treat them like a valued contact, not a lost deal. Provide value. The subtext is "I'm still here, still excellent, zero hard feelings."

Do NOT badmouth the other agent. Ever. Not even subtly. Not even if they chose someone terrible.
Do NOT ask why they didn't choose you (unless you have a close enough relationship that it's natural).
Do NOT say "if it doesn't work out, call me" -- this sounds like you're hoping they fail. Show it through consistent, classy follow-up instead.

Tone:
DO: "Appreciate you letting me know. [Agent name] is solid -- you're in good hands. If you ever need a second opinion on anything down the road, my door's always open. Wishing you a smooth closing."
DON'T: "I'm sorry to hear that. I believe I could have provided you with a higher level of service, but I respect your decision. Please keep me in mind for future transactions."
quickStart: Write 3 messages for when a prospect chooses another agent: a gracious immediate response (150 words), a 90-day check-in text (160 chars), and an annual touchpoint email (120 words). Classy, zero bitterness, door stays open. No badmouthing the other agent.

title: Silent Lead Diagnosis Checklist
bestFor: ** Figuring out WHY a lead went quiet before choosing a re-engagement strategy
prompt:
You are a sales psychologist. Create a diagnostic checklist that helps me figure out why a specific lead went silent, so I can choose the right re-engagement approach.

Design a checklist of 10 yes/no questions I ask myself about the silent lead. Each question should:
1. Point toward a likely reason for silence
2. Include what to do if the answer is "yes"

Cover these potential reasons:
- Life got busy / bad timing
- Found another agent
- Decided not to buy/sell
- Priced out of the market
- Overwhelmed by the process
- I communicated poorly (too much, too little, wrong channel)
- Partner/spouse isn't aligned
- Waiting for market to change
- Financing fell through
- Simply forgot

Format: Numbered checklist. Each item = Question | If Yes, This Is Likely the Reason | Re-Engagement Approach (1 sentence).

Do NOT assume the reason is always "they're just busy." Sometimes the reason is me.
Do NOT recommend the same re-engagement approach for every reason. Different causes need different cures.
Do NOT include reasons I can't assess from available information. Keep it practical.
quickStart: Create a 10-question yes/no diagnostic checklist for figuring out why a lead went silent. Each question maps to a likely reason and a 1-sentence re-engagement approach.

title: "Something Changed" Market Update Re-Engagement
bestFor: ** When a genuine market shift gives you a reason to reach out
prompt:
You are a real estate agent re-engaging a dormant lead with a legitimate market update.

Lead details:
- Name: [NAME]
- Last active: [TIMEFRAME] ago
- Original interest: [BUYING/SELLING, AREA, PRICE RANGE]
- Market change to share: [SPECIFIC CHANGE -- e.g., "interest rates dropped 0.5%," "new development announced," "inventory in their target area doubled"]

Write 3 versions of the re-engagement message:
1. **Text** (under 160 characters)
2. **Email** (under 120 words)
3. **Voicemail script** (under 75 words / 30 seconds)

Each should:
- Lead with the market change, not "checking in"
- Connect the change specifically to their situation
- End with a low-commitment CTA (not "Let's schedule a meeting")

Do NOT pretend you're reaching out casually. Be honest: this specific change made you think of them.
Do NOT oversell the market change. Present the fact, let them decide if it matters.
Do NOT apologize for reaching out. You're sharing useful information.

Tone:
DO: "Quick heads up -- rates just dipped under 6.5%. For the 3-bed range you were looking at in [AREA], that's about $200/month difference from when we last talked. Worth a fresh look?"
DON'T: "Hi [Name], I hope you're well. I wanted to reach out regarding some exciting changes in the market that may impact your home search."
quickStart: Write a text (160 chars), email (120 words), and voicemail (75 words) re-engaging [DORMANT LEAD NAME] using this specific market change: [CHANGE]. Connect it to their situation. Low-commitment CTA.

title: Quick-Question Re-Opener
bestFor: ** Re-starting dialogue with a lead who went quiet
prompt:
You are a real estate agent trying to re-open conversation with a silent lead using one simple question.

Lead details:
- Name: [NAME]
- What they were looking for: [BRIEF DESCRIPTION]
- Last communication: [WHAT AND WHEN]

Write 5 different "quick question" text messages, each designed to be so easy to respond to that they can't resist replying.

Rules for each question:
- Under 160 characters
- Requires a one-word or one-sentence answer
- Relates to their actual situation (not generic)
- Does NOT ask "Are you still looking?" directly

Each question should use a different approach:
1. Either/or choice (binary question)
2. Opinion question (low-stakes, about their area or interests)
3. Timing question (about their timeline, without pressure)
4. Curiosity question (something interesting about the market they'd want to weigh in on)
5. Helpful question (offering something specific)

Do NOT ask questions you don't actually need the answer to. Fake questions feel fake.
Do NOT stack multiple questions. ONE question per message.
Do NOT follow up the same day if they don't respond. Wait at least 5 days.

Tone:
DO: "Random Q -- did you guys end up settling on [AREA] or are you still open to [OTHER AREA]?"
DON'T: "Hi [Name], I wanted to check in on your home search. Are you still interested in purchasing a property?"
quickStart: Write 5 simple, easy-to-answer text questions (under 160 chars each) to re-open conversation with a silent lead [NAME] who was looking for [DESCRIPTION]. No "are you still looking?" -- more creative than that.

title: Success Story Re-Engagement Email
bestFor: ** Leads who stalled due to fear, indecision, or market hesitation
prompt:
You are a real estate agent sharing a client success story to inspire a dormant lead to re-engage.

Dormant lead details:
- Name: [NAME]
- Their situation: [e.g., "first-time buyer who got scared by prices 4 months ago"]
- Likely reason they stalled: [YOUR THEORY]

Success story to share:
- Client profile: [SIMILAR PERSON -- e.g., "another first-time buyer in a similar price range"]
- What happened: [BRIEF OUTCOME -- e.g., "found a great condo $15K under budget after expanding their search area"]
- Key detail that matches the dormant lead's situation: [SPECIFIC PARALLEL]

Write an email (under 120 words) that:
1. Tells the success story briefly (without naming the client)
2. Draws a clear parallel to the dormant lead's situation
3. Doesn't say "this could be you" -- let them connect the dots
4. Ends with a specific, low-pressure CTA

Subject line: Something about the story, not about them being silent.

Do NOT make the success story sound too good to be true.
Do NOT say "I thought of you when..." (overused).
Do NOT include more than one success story. One is a compelling narrative; two is a sales pitch.

Tone:
DO: "A couple I worked with last month was in almost the exact same spot -- priced out, frustrated, about to give up. Then we tried [SPECIFIC STRATEGY] and they closed last Friday."
DON'T: "I've helped many clients in similar situations achieve their real estate goals."
quickStart: Write a 120-word email sharing a client success story to re-engage [DORMANT LEAD NAME] who stalled because [REASON]. Draw a parallel to their situation. Low-pressure CTA. No "this could be you."

title: Trigger Event Re-Engagement Message Set
bestFor: ** When a life or market event creates a natural reason to reach out
prompt:
You are a real estate agent who monitors for natural re-engagement opportunities. Write message templates for 6 different trigger events that create genuine reasons to contact dormant leads.

For each trigger event, write:
1. The trigger (what happened)
2. A text message (under 160 characters)
3. Why this trigger works for re-engagement (1 sentence)

The 6 triggers:
1. **Their neighbor's home just sold** (for a strong price)
2. **A new school, restaurant, or business opened in their target area**
3. **Interest rates changed significantly**
4. **A property matching their old criteria just hit the market**
5. **You noticed a life change on social media** (new job, engagement, baby)
6. **Seasonal timing** (lease expiration season, spring market, year-end)

Do NOT mention you've been "keeping an eye on things for them" -- that sounds like surveillance.
Do NOT use the life-change trigger to make a sales pitch. Lead with genuine congratulations.
Do NOT combine triggers. One reason per message. Clean and clear.

Tone for trigger #5 (life change):
DO: "Congrats on the new gig! That's huge. If it changes anything about the housing search, I'm around. If not -- just wanted to say congrats."
DON'T: "I noticed your career change. This could be a great time to reconsider purchasing a home given your likely increase in income."
quickStart: Write text messages (under 160 chars each) for 6 trigger events that create natural re-engagement opportunities with dormant leads: neighbor sold, new amenity, rate change, matching listing, life change, seasonal timing.

title: Dormant Lead Prioritization Framework
bestFor: ** Deciding which silent leads to pursue first when you can't reach everyone
prompt:
You are a sales strategist. Create a simple scoring framework that helps me prioritize which dormant leads to re-engage first.

I have approximately [NUMBER] dormant leads. I can realistically re-engage [NUMBER] per week.

Design a scoring system with 5 criteria, each scored 1-5:

1. **Recency** -- how recently were they active? (More recent = higher score)
2. **Original engagement depth** -- how far did they get? (Showed homes = higher than just an inquiry)
3. **Trigger events present** -- are there current reasons to reach out? (Yes = higher score)
4. **Estimated transaction value** -- what's the potential commission? (Higher value = higher score)
5. **Re-engagement difficulty** -- how hard will it be? (Easier = higher score)

For each criterion, define what 1, 3, and 5 look like specifically.

Include:
- The scoring table template
- How to calculate the total priority score
- The recommended action for each score range (e.g., 20-25: this week, 15-19: this month, 10-14: this quarter, under 10: long-term nurture only)

Do NOT make the scoring system more than 5 criteria. Simplicity ensures I'll actually use it.
Do NOT suggest pursuing every dormant lead equally. Triage is the point.
Do NOT forget to include "re-engagement difficulty" -- some leads aren't worth the effort regardless of potential value.

Format: Scoring table + action thresholds.
quickStart: Create a 5-criteria scoring system (1-5 each) to prioritize which dormant leads to re-engage first. Criteria: recency, engagement depth, trigger events, transaction value, difficulty. Include action thresholds for score ranges.

title: "Sounds Human" Automation Rules
bestFor: ** Making automated messages indistinguishable from personal ones
prompt:
You are a marketing automation expert who hates robotic automation. Create 10 rules that make automated real estate follow-up messages feel genuinely human.

For each rule:
1. **The rule** (1 sentence)
2. **Why it matters** (1 sentence)
3. **Bad example** (what it looks like without this rule)
4. **Good example** (what it looks like with this rule)

The 10 rules should cover:
- Send time randomization (not sending at exactly 9:00 AM)
- Casual language and strategic imperfection
- Real-time data integration (actual market stats, weather, local events)
- Reply-to functionality (automated emails MUST be reply-able)
- Conversation continuity (reference previous messages)
- Channel mixing (don't send 10 emails in a row)
- Response-triggered pausing (stop automation when they reply)
- Variable message length (not every email the same length)
- Personalization beyond first name
- Time and context awareness (don't send a cheerful message on a holiday when they may not be checking email)

Format: Numbered list, each rule with the 4 sub-components. Keep total output under 500 words.

Do NOT suggest intentional typos in professional communications. "Strategic imperfection" means conversational tone, not sloppy writing.
Do NOT recommend tools or platforms by name. These are principles that work in any system.
Do NOT include rules that require coding or API access. Agents need rules they can implement in standard CRMs.
quickStart: List 10 rules for making automated real estate emails feel human. Each with: the rule, why it matters, a bad example, and a good example. Under 500 words total.

title: New Buyer Lead Automated Action Plan (60 Days)
bestFor: ** Setting up CRM action plans for online buyer leads
prompt:
You are a CRM automation specialist. Design a 60-day automated action plan for a new online buyer lead.

The plan should include:
- **14 automated touches** (emails and texts with complete content)
- **6 human tasks** (phone calls and personal touches that can't be automated -- with scripts or talking points)
- **3 decision branches** (if they respond, if they engage but don't respond, if they go completely silent)

Format as a timeline table:
Day | Type (Auto/Human) | Channel | Content Summary | Full Content | Branch Logic

Rules:
- Automated emails under 120 words each
- Automated texts under 160 characters each
- Phone call talking points under 100 words each
- No more than 3 touches per week
- At least 2 touches should be non-real-estate value
- If they respond at any point, automation pauses and human takes over

Do NOT front-load all touches in week 1. Pace them throughout 60 days.
Do NOT make every touch a CTA. Some should just be value with no ask.
Do NOT automate phone calls. Flag them as tasks that require my personal attention.

Include at the end: the 3 metrics I should track to know if this plan is working (with specific benchmarks).
quickStart: Design a 60-day action plan for new buyer leads with 14 automated touches, 6 human tasks, and 3 decision branches. Timeline table format. All content included. Max 3 touches per week.

title: Past Client Annual Automated Nurture Plan
bestFor: ** Set-and-adjust annual automation for past clients
prompt:
You are a CRM automation specialist. Design a 365-day automated nurture plan for past clients (post-first-year).

My market: [CITY/AREA]

The plan should include:
- **8 automated touches** (emails and texts, with full content)
- **4 human tasks** (calls, pop-bys, or handwritten notes -- with talking points or note content)
- **Seasonal relevance** tied to my market

Format as a timeline:
Month | Type (Auto/Human) | Channel | Content Summary | Full Content

Rules:
- Automated emails under 120 words
- Automated texts under 160 characters
- No more than 1 touch per month (consistency, not volume)
- At least 3 touches should be purely personal -- no real estate content
- Include 1 annual home value update (automated email with placeholder for stats)
- Include 1 referral seed (subtle, embedded naturally)
- The human touches should be high-impact: a call, a pop-by, and a handwritten note

Do NOT create a plan that feels like a drip campaign. Past clients should feel remembered, not marketed to.
Do NOT schedule automated touches within 3 days of a human task. Space them out.
Do NOT automate the handwritten note -- just flag it as a task with the content to write.
quickStart: Design a 12-month past client nurture plan with 8 automated touches and 4 human tasks. One touch per month, mixed channels, mostly personal. Full content included.

title: Automation Pause and Resume Rules
bestFor: ** Preventing awkward automated messages after a client responds
prompt:
You are a CRM workflow specialist. Create a clear set of rules for when to pause, resume, and permanently stop automated follow-up sequences.

Design rules for 3 scenarios:

**1. PAUSE automation when:**
List 5 specific triggers (e.g., client replies, client calls, meeting scheduled).
For each: what happens during the pause and how long it lasts.

**2. RESUME automation when:**
List 4 conditions for restarting a paused sequence.
For each: where in the sequence to restart (from the beginning, where they left off, or skip ahead).

**3. PERMANENTLY STOP automation when:**
List 4 conditions that mean this contact should never receive automated follow-up again.
For each: what manual process (if any) replaces the automation.

Format: Three sections with numbered rules. Keep total output under 300 words.

Do NOT resume automation within 48 hours of a human interaction. It feels jarring.
Do NOT permanently stop automation just because someone didn't respond. Silence isn't rejection.
Do NOT create rules so complex they require a flowchart to follow. Simple rules get followed.
quickStart: Create simple rules for when to pause, resume, and permanently stop automated follow-up. 5 pause triggers, 4 resume conditions, 4 stop conditions. Under 300 words total.

title: Monthly Automation Performance Audit Prompt
bestFor: ** Monthly review of automated follow-up effectiveness
prompt:
You are my automation performance analyst. I'm going to paste my email/text automation stats for the past month. Analyze them and tell me what to fix.

Here are my stats:
- [SEQUENCE NAME]: [OPEN RATE]%, [REPLY RATE]%, [UNSUBSCRIBE RATE]%, [EMAILS IN SEQUENCE]
- [REPEAT FOR EACH ACTIVE SEQUENCE]

Overall database: [NUMBER] contacts, [NUMBER] active in sequences

Analyze and provide:

1. **Health check** (3 sentences): Overall assessment. Am I in good shape or trouble?
2. **Worst performer**: Which sequence needs the most urgent attention? What specifically is wrong?
3. **Best performer**: Which sequence is working? What can I learn from it?
4. **3 specific fixes**: Concrete changes to make this month (not vague advice like "improve your subject lines" -- tell me HOW)
5. **1 test to run**: The single most valuable A/B test I should start based on this data

Format: Numbered sections as listed above. Keep total output under 400 words.

Do NOT tell me everything is fine if my open rates are below 25%. That's a problem.
Do NOT suggest rewriting everything at once. Prioritize the highest-impact change.
Do NOT ignore unsubscribe rates. Even 1% per email is a signal.
quickStart: Analyze my automation stats: [PASTE STATS]. Tell me: overall health (3 sentences), worst performer, best performer, 3 specific fixes, and 1 A/B test to run. Under 400 words.

title: Multi-Channel Follow-Up Sequence for New Leads (14 Days)
bestFor: ** Building an omnipresent but not annoying first impression
prompt:
You are a multi-channel sales strategist. Design a 14-day follow-up sequence for a new lead that uses multiple channels strategically.

Lead source: [WHERE THE LEAD CAME FROM -- e.g., "online inquiry," "referral," "met at community event"]
My available channels: email, text, phone, video message, social media, handwritten note

Design the sequence with 8-10 touchpoints over 14 days:

For each touchpoint, provide:
| Day | Time | Channel | Content (full text) | Purpose (1 sentence) |

Channel distribution rules:
- No channel used more than 3 times
- No two consecutive touches on the same channel
- Phone calls maximum 2 (with voicemail scripts, under 75 words each)
- Texts under 160 characters
- Emails under 120 words
- Include at least 1 non-real-estate touchpoint
- Include 1 video message script (under 60 seconds / 150 words)
- Include 1 handwritten note (if lead is high-value or referral)

Do NOT front-load everything in days 1-3. Spread touches across all 14 days.
Do NOT send a text AND an email on the same day.
Do NOT make every touchpoint a CTA. Some should just build familiarity and trust.

Tone across all channels:
DO: Feel like one person reaching out through different natural channels
DON'T: Feel like a coordinated marketing campaign hitting them from all angles
quickStart: Design a 14-day, 8-10 touchpoint follow-up sequence for a new [LEAD SOURCE] lead using email, text, phone, video, social, and handwritten note. Full content for each touch. No channel used more than 3 times.

title: Channel Selection Decision Tree
bestFor: ** Knowing which channel to use for any follow-up situation
prompt:
You are a communication strategist. Create a simple decision tree that helps me choose the right follow-up channel for any situation.

The decision tree should have 4 decision points:
1. **How urgent is this?** (Time-sensitive vs. can wait)
2. **How deep is the relationship?** (New lead vs. active client vs. past client)
3. **What's the message type?** (Quick update, detailed info, emotional/personal, or logistical)
4. **What's their preference?** (Known preference overrides everything)

For each terminal node (the final recommendation), specify:
- The recommended channel
- The backup channel if the first isn't possible
- A 1-sentence rationale

Include a quick-reference summary table at the end:
| Situation | Best Channel | Why |

Cover at least 10 common situations in the summary table.

Do NOT make the decision tree more than 4 levels deep. If I need a flowchart app to follow it, it's too complex.
Do NOT forget that client preference always wins. Build that as the first branch.
Do NOT recommend phone calls for quick updates. That's what texts are for.

Keep total output under 400 words.
quickStart: Create a 4-question decision tree for choosing the right follow-up channel (email, text, phone, video, DM, mail). Include a 10-row summary table of common situations and recommended channels.

title: Escalation and De-Escalation Communication Protocols
bestFor: ** Knowing when to intensify or back off follow-up
prompt:
You are a sales communication strategist. Create clear escalation and de-escalation protocols for real estate follow-up.

**ESCALATION** (moving to more personal, higher-touch channels):
List 5 triggers that mean I should escalate. For each:
- The trigger signal
- What to do (specific channel shift and message approach)
- Example message (under 100 words)

**DE-ESCALATION** (backing off to lighter, less frequent touches):
List 5 triggers that mean I should de-escalate. For each:
- The trigger signal
- What to do (specific channel shift and frequency change)
- Example message (under 100 words)

**THE COOLING-OFF PROTOCOL:**
When I've been too aggressive and a client signals annoyance, write:
1. The acknowledgment message (under 100 words -- own it, reset expectations)
2. The waiting period (how long to go silent)
3. The re-entry message (under 100 words -- lighter, zero pressure)

Do NOT frame de-escalation as giving up. It's strategic patience.
Do NOT make the cooling-off protocol groveling. One honest message, then back off.
Do NOT escalate past phone calls. Showing up unannounced is never appropriate.

Keep total output under 500 words.
quickStart: Create escalation rules (5 triggers to intensify follow-up), de-escalation rules (5 triggers to back off), and a cooling-off protocol for when I've been too aggressive. Include example messages. Under 500 words.

title: Critical Moment Follow-Up Message Library
bestFor: ** Building a go-to library for the moments that matter most
prompt:
You are a real estate communication specialist. Write follow-up messages for the 15 most critical moments in the client journey.

For each moment, write:
1. **The text version** (under 160 characters)
2. **The email version** (under 120 words)
3. **When to send it** (specific timing)

The 15 critical moments:
1. After the initial buyer consultation
2. After the initial seller consultation
3. After submitting an offer (to the buyer)
4. Offer accepted -- congratulations
5. After home inspection (issues found)
6. After a low appraisal
7. Clear to close notification
8. Morning of closing day
9. Day after closing
10. One week post-closing
11. 30 days post-closing
12. When a neighbor's home sells
13. When market conditions shift significantly
14. After receiving a referral from a past client
15. Annual "how's your home" check-in

Do NOT use the same opening formula for more than 3 messages. Vary your approach.
Do NOT make every message feel like a milestone -- some should feel casual and human.
Do NOT include CTAs in moments that should just be emotional (like closing day morning).

Tone guardrails:
- Moments 1-7: Professional warmth. They need confidence in you.
- Moments 8-11: Pure celebration and care. Zero business talk.
- Moments 12-15: Friendly expert. Value-driven, relationship-focused.

This library becomes my go-to resource so I never stare at a blank screen wondering what to say.
quickStart: Write text (160 chars) and email (120 words) versions for 15 critical client journey moments: consultations, offer, inspection, appraisal, closing, post-close milestones, and ongoing check-ins. Include send timing for each.

title: Quarterly Follow-Up System Self-Assessment
bestFor: ** Quarterly review to ensure your follow-up system is actually working
variables: LIST WITH APPROXIMATE PERCENTAGE OF EACH
prompt:
You are my follow-up accountability partner. I'm going to share my quarterly follow-up performance data. Analyze it and grade my system.

Here's my data:
- Total contacts in database: [NUMBER]
- Contacts touched in last 90 days: [NUMBER]
- Response rate (replies / outreach sent): [PERCENTAGE]%
- Referrals received this quarter: [NUMBER]
- Referral sources (which past clients referred): [LIST OR NUMBER]
- Leads that went silent this quarter: [NUMBER]
- Transactions closed from follow-up (not new leads): [NUMBER]
- Channels used: [LIST WITH APPROXIMATE PERCENTAGE OF EACH]
- Biggest follow-up win this quarter: [DESCRIBE]
- Biggest follow-up miss: [DESCRIBE]

Provide:

1. **Overall grade** (A through F) with a 2-sentence justification
2. **Coverage score**: What percentage of my database did I actually touch? Is that enough?
3. **Response quality**: Is my response rate healthy? What benchmark should I target?
4. **Referral health**: Am I generating enough referrals per contact? What's the gap?
5. **Channel balance**: Am I over-relying on one channel? What should I adjust?
6. **Top 3 priorities for next quarter**: Specific, actionable, with a measurable target for each

Format: Numbered sections as listed. Keep total output under 400 words. Be honest -- I need truth, not encouragement.

Do NOT grade on a curve. Use objective benchmarks.
Do NOT give me more than 3 priorities. Focus beats ambition.
Do NOT suggest adding more tools or technology unless my current tools are maxed out.
quickStart: Grade my follow-up system (A-F) based on: [NUMBER] contacts, [NUMBER] touched in 90 days, [PERCENTAGE]% response rate, [NUMBER] referrals. Give me 3 priorities for next quarter with measurable targets. Be honest.

title: Should I Hold This Property Open? (The Decision Framework)
bestFor: ** Agents choosing between multiple listings to hold open
prompt:
You are a real estate open house strategist. Help me decide whether a specific property is worth holding open.

Here are the details for the property I am considering:
- Address & neighborhood: [ADDRESS / NEIGHBORHOOD]
- Property type & price: [BEDS/BATHS/SQFT, $PRICE]
- Location traffic: [HIGH / MODERATE / LOW drive-by and foot traffic]
- Neighborhood density: [NUMBER of homes within 0.5 miles]
- Days on market: [NUMBER]
- Are nearby homeowners likely sellers? [YES / NO / UNSURE — and why]
- How photogenic is the property for social content? [1-10]

Score this property on a 1-10 scale across these 6 criteria, then give me a HOLD OPEN or SKIP recommendation with reasoning:

| Criteria | Score (1-10) | Notes |
|----------|-------------|-------|
| Drive-by / foot traffic potential | | |
| Neighborhood seller lead potential | | |
| Price point attractiveness (draws volume) | | |
| Social media content potential | | |
| My personal brand-building in this area | | |
| Likelihood of generating buyer leads beyond this listing | | |

Total score interpretation: 40+ = must hold open. 30-39 = hold open if schedule allows. Below 30 = skip unless strategic reason.

Do NOT give a generic "every open house is worth it" answer — be honest about when my time is better spent elsewhere.
Do NOT factor in the likelihood of selling THIS property at the open house — that is a bonus, not the goal.
Do NOT recommend holding open houses in areas where I have no intention of building a long-term presence.
quickStart: I'm deciding whether to hold an open house at [ADDRESS] — a [PROPERTY DESCRIPTION] at $[PRICE]. Score it 1-10 on: traffic potential, neighborhood seller leads, buyer lead volume, and content potential. Give me a HOLD or SKIP recommendation.

title: Setting Specific Goals for This Open House
bestFor: ** Every agent, before every open house
prompt:
You are a real estate performance coach. I am hosting an open house and need specific, measurable goals so I can evaluate whether it was successful.

Here is my context:
- Property: [ADDRESS], [BEDS/BATHS/SQFT] at $[PRICE]
- Date/time: [DATE, TIME RANGE]
- My typical open house results: [NUMBER] visitors, [NUMBER] sign-ins, [NUMBER] meaningful conversations
- Neighborhood: [DESCRIPTION — suburban, urban, rural; density; homeowner demographics]
- My marketing plan intensity: [MINIMAL / MODERATE / AGGRESSIVE]

Create a goal sheet I can print and bring to the open house in this exact format:

**OPEN HOUSE GOALS — [DATE]**

| Metric | Target | Actual | Notes |
|--------|--------|--------|-------|
| Total visitors | | | |
| Sign-in completions | | | |
| Sign-in rate (%) | | | |
| Serious buyer conversations | | | |
| Neighbor conversations | | | |
| Appointments or callbacks scheduled | | | |
| Business cards / contact info collected beyond sign-in | | | |
| Social content pieces captured | | | |

Set targets that are 25-50% above my typical results — ambitious but realistic. Below each target, write one sentence explaining HOW to hit it.

Do NOT set vague goals like "have a great open house" — every goal must be a number.
Do NOT include follow-up goals here — that belongs to my post-event process.
Do NOT set visitor targets that are unrealistic for my neighborhood density and marketing level.
quickStart: I typically get [NUMBER] visitors at open houses. Give me a goal sheet table with 8 measurable targets that push me 25-50% above those numbers for my open house at [ADDRESS] on [DATE].

title: 14-Day Open House Countdown Checklist
bestFor: ** Agents who want a day-by-day action plan
variables: SOLO / HAVE AN ASSISTANT / CO-HOSTING WITH LENDER OR PARTNER
prompt:
